items.title: Deployment Frequency Dynamics: How Release Cadence Shapes Quality Strategy
items.author: Chamelo Ai
items.content:
In today’s software development landscape, deployment frequency has emerged as a critical strategic decision that fundamentally shapes how teams approach quality. Organizations operate across a spectrum of release cadences — from multiple deployments per day to quarterly releases — with each point on this continuum demanding its own quality engineering approach.

This guide is particularly valuable for engineering managers, QA leaders, and DevOps practitioners who are evaluating or planning changes to their organization’s deployment cadence. CTOs and technical directors will also find strategic insights to inform broader digital transformation initiatives.

The Deployment Frequency Spectrum

Deployment frequency isn’t merely a scheduling decision; it’s a strategic choice that influences every aspect of your software delivery process. Let’s explore how quality strategy must adapt across different deployment cadences:

Continuous Deployment (Multiple Times Daily)

  • Small, incremental changes
  • Automated deployment pipelines
  • Lower risk per individual change
  • Higher cumulative operational demands

Quality Strategy Implications:

  • Automation is non-negotiable: Near 100% test automation coverage of critical paths
  • Monitoring becomes testing: Heavy investment in observability and alerting
  • Resilience over prevention: Rapid rollback capabilities and fault tolerance
  • Developers must own significant testing responsibility
  • Test selection algorithms: Smart determination of which tests to run for each change (leverage AI for this — to be followed in future case studies)

Engineering Excellence 🐐 Rating: If your team operates at a continuous deployment cadence, your team is a . Legendary excellence, setting the standard that others aspire to reach.

  • Single batch of changes each day
  • Slightly larger change scope
  • End-of-day deployment rituals

Quality Strategy Implications:

  • Testing happens throughout the day leading to deployment
  • Unit tests run continuously, integration and E2E tests before deployment
  • Post-deployment verification becomes critical
  • Standard issues addressed in next day’s deployment, with critical bugs handled via immediate hot-fix process
  • All significant changes must be toggle-controlled

Engineering Excellence 🐐 Rating: If your team deploys once per day, it’s a . Versatile excellence with remarkable consistency and adaptability.

  • Moderate batch size of changes
  • Weekly deployment ceremonies
  • Time for more thorough testing
  • Balance between speed and stability

Quality Strategy Implications:

  • Dedicated testing windows: Specific time allocated for focused testing
  • Formal staging environments: Realistic pre-production validation
  • Blend of automation and exploratory testing: Time for both approaches
  • Performance testing for each release: Sufficient scope to warrant performance validation
  • Ensuring accumulated changes don’t break existing functionality

Engineering Excellence 🐐 Rating: If your team operates on a weekly deployment schedule, it’s a . Disciplined excellence with methodical precision and room to push boundaries.

  • More stakeholders involved
  • Higher risk per deployment

Quality Strategy Implications:

  • Comprehensive regression testing: Full validation of core functionality
  • Time for business stakeholders to validate
  • Early access for select users before full release
  • Formal test case management: More structured approach to test coverage
  • Change advisory processes: Formal review of changes and test results

Engineering Excellence 🐐 Rating: If your team ships code every two weeks, it’s a . Powerful performer with tremendous skills, poised to take the next step toward championship level.

  • Major feature accumulation
  • Extended stabilization periods
  • Significant organizational coordination

Quality Strategy Implications:

  • Full-cycle testing approach: Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing phases
  • Non-functional testing emphasis: Security, performance, accessibility, etc.
  • Test plans, test cases, and results documentation
  • Go/no-go decision processes: Formal release readiness assessment

Engineering Excellence 🐐 Rating: If your team delivers software on a quarterly schedule, it’s a . Immense potential with raw power, ready for the coaching that will transform you into a dominant force.

QA Role Distribution Across Deployment Frequencies

Deployment frequency significantly impacts how QE roles are distributed. Note that these roles aren’t necessarily siloed into a separate “QA Team” — QAs and SDETs are often embedded directly within engineering teams or pods. The following graph illustrates this distribution across the deployment spectrum:

  • SDETs and automation engineers dominate in high-frequency deployment models
  • Manual QA testers become increasingly prevalent as deployment frequency decreases
  • Specialized testing roles (performance, security, accessibility) grow significantly in less frequent deployment models
  • The crossover point occurs around weekly deployments, where manual and automated testing reach relative balance

This shift reflects the different testing strategies required at each deployment frequency. High-frequency deployments depend on automation and developer-owned testing, while less frequent deployments allow time for specialized manual testing approaches.

Factors Influencing Optimal Deployment Frequency

Determining the right deployment frequency isn’t one-size-fits-all. Consider these key factors:

  1. System Criticality and Risk Profile: Mission-critical systems may warrant less frequent, more controlled release cycles, while lower-risk systems can benefit from faster frequencies.
  2. Teams with strong engineering practices and automation can support faster deployment cycles, though smaller teams may struggle with continuous deployment overhead.
  3. Microservices typically enable more frequent, independent deployments, while monolithic systems often benefit from more controlled release cadences.
  4. Organizational Structure: Companies with strong cross-functional alignment support faster cycles, while siloed organizations require longer coordination periods.
  5. Some user bases expect stability and predictability, while others value rapid innovation and frequent improvements.
  6. Heavily regulated industries may require documentation, approvals, and verification steps that impact feasible deployment frequencies.

Quality Engineering Principles Across the Spectrum

Regardless of deployment frequency, these quality engineering principles remain constant:

  1. Continuously increase test automation coverage, with its urgency increasing with deployment frequency.
  2. Quality Is Everyone’s Responsibility: Cultivate shared ownership of quality at any cadence.
  3. Observability Complements Testing: Production monitoring becomes increasingly critical as deployment frequency increases.
  4. Balance Leading and Lagging Indicators: Track both preventative metrics and outcome metrics.
  5. Continuously Reassess Position: Regularly reevaluate whether your current deployment frequency remains optimal.

Transitioning Between Deployment Frequencies

Many organizations aim to increase their deployment frequency over time, requiring deliberate quality strategy adjustments:

Moving from Quarterly to Monthly (Rookie Shaq to Charles Barkley)

  • Start with smaller, less complex releases
  • Increase automation coverage of regression scenarios
  • Streamline approval processes
  • Split test teams to support parallel release cycles

Moving from Bi-Weekly to Weekly (Charles Barkley to Kobe Bryant)

  • Implement feature flagging infrastructure
  • Enhance CI/CD pipeline maturity
  • Shift from comprehensive to risk-based testing
  • Build developer testing capabilities

Moving from Weekly to Daily (Kobe Bryant to LeBron James)

  • Achieve near-complete automation of critical paths
  • Implement trunk-based development practices
  • Develop fast feedback mechanisms
  • Enhance monitoring and alerting

Moving from Daily to Continuous (LeBron James to Michael Jordan)

  • Implement canary or blue-green deployment capabilities
  • Develop advanced monitoring and auto-remediation
  • Create sophisticated test selection algorithms
  • Establish strong engineering quality culture

Many organizations effectively implement hybrid deployment models:

  • Component-Based Frequencies: Core infrastructure might deploy monthly, while customer-facing features deploy continuously
  • Environment-Based Progression: Continuous deployment to development and staging, with coordinated production releases
  • Simple changes flow continuously, while complex changes batch into scheduled releases

Your deployment frequency is more than just a schedule — it’s a strategic choice that shapes quality practices throughout your organization. The most successful organizations align their deployment frequency with their quality strategy. As one of the four key DORA metrics, deployment frequency correlates with high performance but must be balanced with lead time, change failure rate, and time to restore service.

In the next post, we’ll explore “DevOps and Quality Engineering: Building a Seamless Integration,” examining how DevOps culture and practices enable your chosen deployment frequency through CI/CD implementation patterns and quality gates that support rather than hinder your optimal cadence.

items.author: Trevor Boodram
items.content:
Hello Everyone and welcome to another documentation post today I will be going over on creating Os Ticket from not having Os ticket installed to be able to create tickets using Azure Virtual Machines.Ticket systems keeps tasks organized in one system, by prioritizing the tickets for teams so it’s easier to see who is handling which ticket.

I first stated signing into Azure portal can we want to create a virtual machine.

Next we want to download Remote Desktop so we can connect to our Virtual Machine.

We copy the the public IP that we get from Azure virtual machine and paste it into the PC name and then click on save.

Next we grab the os ticket zip file and download it .

Next we go to control panel and click on programs to uninstall a program.

We want to turn on Internet Information Systems and CGI and hit OK to apply changes.

Next we load the IIS deaflault webpage

Next I installed PHP manager.

Next we installed IIS ReWrite which allows us to manage the URLs on the web server.

Next I opened and created a PHP file.

Then I uploaded the default a PHP folder and I want in my C drive PHP.

Then we can install Microsoft Visual this will allow us to get access to different Microsoft tool and technologies for developing applications or software.

Next we installed MySQL server 5.5 amd this will allow for our storage database store our user accounts, different variety of tickets, and updates.This will be on the backend.Once we setup on standard configuration we will create our root user so I added a the root user password.

Next we open up IIS and click on PHP manger and this will have our web server aware of PHP.We choose the three dots where we will find the executable file path for PHP.

Next we want to drag the upload folder to the root folder and rename it to osTicket

Now we go back to IIS and click on sites and we can now open up os Ticket webpage successfully.

We can see that certain extensions were not installed on os ticket so we enable them by going to IIS and PHP extensions.

Next we can reload OsTicket and we can see that the check marks have been checked and installed.

Next we want to go back to the root folder and we want to change the name form ost-sampleconfig.php To ost-config.php. We want to also take away the standard permissions.

Next we want to add our own permissions and click ok. Also check off only full control and apply changes.

Next we can enter our credentials and also create the user.

Then we install Heidi SQL this in an application that will make a connection to our database.

Next we want to click on new and provide the information to make our connection to the database.Also for the database name I called it osTicket. Now we have a database called osTicket.

Next we go back to the osTicket tab and enter the database information that we created.

After that we can click on install now and Os Ticket is now up and running.

Now we can see in IIS the osticket database that all the tables have been created that is part of OS ticket website.

Thank you for taking your time to read through my documentation as I went over creating azure virtual machine and installing OS ticket.A Ticketing system bring great value because it helps businesses focus on more urgent related issues by assigning the ticket to individuals they can prioritize and there problems effectively.

items.title: Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) in Kubernetes: Build Your Own API
items.author: Shivam Kumar
items.content:
Before diving into CRDs one should know what is. Kubernetes resources is an endpoint in the Kubernetes API that stores a collection of API objects.

Resource is an object that extends the Kubernetes API or allows you to introduce your own API into a cluster.

What is a CustomResourceDefinition(CRD)

A CustomResourceDefinition (CRD) defines the name and structure of your custom resource that you want to add to the cluster.

CRDs allow users to create new types of custom resources without adding another API server. When you create a new CRD, the Kubernetes API Server creates a new RESTful resource path to serve and handle the storage of your custom resource.

I will be using Minikube for this demo.

If you run the below command you will get error.

Kubectl get testerror: the server doesn't have a resource type "test"

This is expected test is not a valid resource name that comes out of the box in the K8S API. I first need to add the testobject to my K8S API using a CRD.

Creating a CustomResourceDefinition

Let’s create our own CustomResourceDefinition(CRD). Save this YAML mainfest in a file and apply it using the command 

apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1kind: CustomResourceDefinition  # name must be in the form: <plural>.<group>spec:
  # group name to use for REST API: /apis/<group>/<version>
  group: example.com  names:
    # kind is normally the CamelCased singular type. 
    kind: MyApp
    # singular name to be used as an alias on the CLI
    singular: myapp
    # plural name in the URL: /apis/<group>/<version>/<plural>
    plural: myapps  - name: v1    storage: true      openAPIV3Schema:        properties:            x-kubernetes-preserve-unknown-fields: true

When applying output comes-

kubectl apply -f crd.ymlcustomresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/myapps.example.com created

and when checking the CRD by running the command-

kubectl get crdNAME CREATED AT
myapps.example.com 2025–03–28T02:55:49Z

Now we have created our own CRD and we will create custom object now.

Let’s create our own Custom Object.To create the custom object, we will use this YAML manifest-

apiVersion: example.com/v1kind: MyApp  name: test-app  replicas: 1  version: release-1.0.0

When saving the above mainfest, applying using kubectl command then running the following command-

kubectl get myapp        NAME       AGE

Deleting CustomResourceDefinition

In the cleanup part we will delete our CRD using the below command-

kubectl delete crd myapps.example.comcustomresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "myapps.example.com" deleted

Now running will throw error

Error from server (NotFound): Unable to list "example.com/v1, Resource=myapps": the server could not find the requested resource (get myapps.example.com)

Overall Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) extend Kubernetes by allowing users to define their own API resources.

items.title: Mastering Git Version Control: A Hands-On Guide with a Mini Project
items.author: Naman Sharma
items.content:
Git is a version control system that helps us manage our code efficiently. It consists of different areas, including the working directory and the staging area. Any changes to the code, such as editing, testing, and packaging, can be tracked using Git.

Let us understand git commands with the help of a small project.

Step 1: Initialize a Git Repository ,First, create a directory where you want to store your project.

 mkdir gitproject cd gitproject/

Step 2: Create and add three files .

index.html → Main HTML file

style.css → CSS for styling

README.md → Documentation

touch index.htmltouch style.cssvim style.css
<!DOCTYPE html><head>    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Git Practice Project</title>
</head>    <h1>Welcome to Git Practice!</h1>
    <p>This is a simple web page to practice Git commands.</p>
</body>
-----------------------------------------------
style.css    font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
    background-color: #f0f0f0;
    color: #333;    color: #007BFF;-----------------------------------------------
This is a simple project to practice Git commands and workflows.
- `index.html`: Basic HTML file
- `style.css`: Styling for the HTML page

Step 3: Add Files to Staging Area ,before committing changes, we must add them to the staging area, where Git keeps track of them.

Step 4: Create Initial Commit ,A commit saves all staged changes in the Git history.

git commit -m "Initial Commit"

Step 5: Check Git Logs , viewing logs helps track previous commits and find commit IDs for reference.

Step 6: Create and Switch to a New Branch. Branching allows working on a new feature without affecting the main code.

Step 7: Create app.js in the Feature BranchWe create app.js in the feature-branch and commit the changes.

At this point, we now have two branches:

master (original branch with 3 files)
feature-branch (now has app.js)
app.js
console.log('Hello, Git!');

Step 8: Merging Feature Branch into Master branch.

Let us first understand how merge works and what is difference between git merge and rebase.

Merge is used to combine the changes from one branch into the another. Creates a merge commit, recording the history of both branches.

Rebase (git rebase feature-branch) : Moves commits from feature-branch on top of master. Does not create a merge commit instead, it rewrites commit history.

Happens when master has no new commits, and feature-branch has progressed ahead. Git simply moves the branch pointer forward instead of creating a merge commit.

git switch mastergit merge  feature

Step 9: Stashing Changes : If you have uncommitted changes but you don’t want to commit yet, git stash temporarily saves them. Later, you can apply them back with git stash pop. Example : You have made any changes in index.html which is present in the feature branch what you do not want to commit changes right now so here comes the role of git stash command.

git stash

Step 10: Different Reset Commands in Git1. git reset hard : Rolls back the last commit, removing all changes from both staging and working areas.

2. git reset mixed : Moves the HEAD pointer to the previous commit but keeps changes in the working directory.

3. git reset soft : Moves the HEAD pointer back but keeps all changes staged.

git reset --hard HEAD~1
git reset --mixed  HEAD~1

Step 11: Other Git CommandsCherry-pick (git cherry-pick <commit-id>)Selectively applies changes from a specific commit to another branch.

Clone : Downloads a remote repository to your local machine.

Fork : (GitHub/GitLab feature, not a Git command) .Creates a separate copy of a repository in your GitHub/GitLab account.

git clone <repo url>
  1. When you create a file in a feature branch but do not commit it, the file will remain in your working directory as an uncommitted change. If you then switch to the master branch, that file will still be there, but it will appear as an uncommitted change (a modification or addition) in your working directory. This behavior occurs because the uncommitted file is part of your working directory, and Git doesn’t delete uncommitted changes when switching branches. However, if the file you’re working on is already tracked and changes conflict between branches, Git might warn you or prevent the switch until you’ve resolved those changes.
  2. By default we are in master or main branch.
  3. To understand how merge or rebase work try viewing logs.
  4. To understand how git revert work try different examples create test files as see what happen when you revert back to previous commit id.

Some Questions to analyze :

  1. In the above mentioned screen shot we can see that currently we are in directory gitpractise.
  2. We are in feature-abc branch.
  3. inside feature branch we have gitproject/ directory.
  4. To maintain a clean and organized workflow, it’s better to create new projects outside of feature branches unless it’s directly related to the feature you’re working on. If you do need to include a new project, consider using Git submodules or ensuring the project structure doesn’t interfere with your feature development.

Thank You for reading………………

items.title: Patching Ingress-nginx CVE-2025–1974 without loosing your mind
items.author: Federico Abihaggle
items.content:
As you may already know, on Monday, March 24, 2025 the ingress-nginx maintainers have released patches for a set of critical vulnerabilities that could allow attackers to easily compromise your Kubernetes cluster.

One of this patches solves CVE-2025–1974 which has a CVSS score of 9.8. As a result, many users are updating their ingress-nginx controllers version to v1.12.1, the version that mitigates this and others vulnerabilities.

Updating Ingress-NGINX: What You Need to Know

You can follow the recommended update process without any issues. If you’re on version v1.21.0 (Helm chart version 4.12.0), simply review the changelog and proceed with the upgrade.

However, if you’re running v1.11.5 (Helm chart version 4.11.5) or earlier, be aware of some breaking changes introduced in v1.21.0 (as noted in the changelog):

⚠️ Enable security features by default (#11819) ⚠️
This changes the default of the following CLI arguments:
--enable-annotation-validation gets enabled by default.
It also changes the default of the following configuration options:
allow-cross-namespace-resources gets disabled by default.
annotations-risk-level gets lowered to "High" by default.
strict-validate-path-type gets enabled by default.

One particularly impactful change is the annotations-risk-levelsetting, which was lowered from Critical to High by default. If you're affected by this change, you should avoid using the following annotations in your :

    One common annotation to use is configuration-snippet, using this annotation you can add additional configuration to the NGINX location.

    If you can’t avoid using these annotations, you can override the default annotations-risk-level and set it back to by adding the following configuration in your Helm values:

    controller:  config:
        annotations-risk-level: Critical

    Common issues if you have these annotations

    If you’ve recently upgraded and are experiencing issues, you might be affected by the annotations-risk-level change. Some common symptoms are:

    • After upgrade your ingress don’t have loadBalancer configuration (don’t have an address assigned):
    kubectl get ingress <ingress-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer}'{}
    • Creating a new ingress you have an error like:
    "ingress.yaml": error when patching "ingress.yaml": admission webhook "validate.nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io" denied the request: annotation group ConfigurationSnippet contains risky annotation based on ingress configuration
    "ingress.yaml": error when patching "ingress.yaml": admission webhook "validate.nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io" denied the request: annotation group AuthSnippet contains risky annotation based on ingress configuration
    "ingress.yaml": error when patching "ingress.yaml": admission webhook "validate.nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io" denied the request: annotation group ModSecuritySnippet contains risky annotation based on ingress configuration
    "ingress.yaml": error when patching "ingress.yaml": admission webhook "validate.nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io" denied the request: annotation group ServerSnippet contains risky annotation based on ingress configuration
    "ingress.yaml": error when patching "ingress.yaml": admission webhook "validate.nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io" denied the request: annotation group StreamSnippet contains risky annotation based on ingress configuration
    • ✅ Patching security vulnerabilities is crucial — but always review the changes before applying them.
    • 🚨 Never update production directly! Always test changes in a staging environment before promoting them.
    • 📖 Read the changelogs carefully to understand how updates may impact your setup.

    🎉 Stay secure, and happy patching! 🎉

    Feel free to share any other issues you’ve run into after upgrading the NGINX Ingress Controller.

items.title: The Ultimate Guide to Optimizing ProxySQL for Peak Performance
items.author: Aman Singh
items.content:
ProxySQL is a powerful, high-performance SQL proxy that sits between your application and database servers, helping to manage connections, route queries efficiently, and improve overall database performance. However, like any tool, ProxySQL needs proper tuning to unleash its full potential.

In this guide, we’ll explore personalized optimization techniques to make your ProxySQL server blazing fast, resilient, and perfectly tuned for your workload — whether you’re handling high-traffic OLTP applications, read-heavy analytics, or a mixed workload.

1. Understanding Your Workload

Before diving into optimizations, know your traffic patterns:

  • OLTP (Transactional Workloads) — High-frequency, short-lived queries (e.g., e-commerce, banking)
  • OLAP (Analytical Workloads) — Long-running, complex queries (e.g., reporting, BI tools)
  •  — A combination of both

Use ProxySQL’s built-in to analyze traffic:

SELECT hostgroup, digest, count_star, sum_time, substr(digest_text, 1, 100) FROM stats_mysql_query_digest 
ORDER BY sum_time DESC LIMIT 10;

This reveals your slowest and most frequent queries, helping you optimize routing and caching.

2. Connection Pooling: The Heart of ProxySQL

ProxySQL’s connection pooling reduces database load by reusing connections. Optimize it with:

-- Reduce connection churnUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='300000' WHERE variable_name='mysql-connection_max_age_ms'; -- 5min
-- Prevent connection stormsUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='100' WHERE variable_name='mysql-connect_retries_on_failure';
-- Timeout adjustmentsUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='5000' WHERE variable_name='mysql-connect_timeout_server';
UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='30000' WHERE variable_name='mysql-default_query_timeout';
-- Load to runtime & persistLOAD MYSQL VARIABLES TO RUNTIME;
SAVE MYSQL VARIABLES TO DISK;
  • If you see max_connections errors in MySQL, increase ProxySQL’s pooling to reduce backend strain.
  • Use SHOW PROCESSLIST; in MySQL to verify connection reuse.

3. Query Caching for Read-Heavy Workloads

If your workload is , enable to avoid hitting the database repeatedly:

-- Enable caching for SELECTs (excluding sensitive queries)INSERT INTO mysql_query_rules (rule_id, active, match_pattern, cache_ttl, apply)   (10, 1, '^SELECT.*FROM orders WHERE', 5000, 1),
  (11, 1, '^SELECT.*FROM products', 10000, 1);
-- Configure cache size (adjust based on RAM)UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='true' WHERE variable_name='mysql-query_cache_enabled';
UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='1024' WHERE variable_name='mysql-query_cache_size_MB'; -- 1GB
LOAD MYSQL QUERY RULES TO RUNTIME;SAVE MYSQL QUERY RULES TO DISK;
  • Frequently updated tables
  • Queries with NOW(), RAND(), or user-specific data

4. Smart Read/Write Splitting

ProxySQL can automatically route writes to primaries and reads to replicas. Define rules like this:

-- Route writes to HG10 (primary)INSERT INTO mysql_query_rules (rule_id, active, match_pattern, destination_hostgroup, apply)   (1, 1, '^INSERT', 10, 1),
  (2, 1, '^UPDATE', 10, 1),
  (3, 1, '^DELETE', 10, 1);
-- Route reads to HG20 (replicas)INSERT INTO mysql_query_rules (rule_id, active, match_pattern, destination_hostgroup, apply)   (4, 1, '^SELECT', 20, 1);
-- Exclude transactions from splittingINSERT INTO mysql_query_rules (rule_id, active, match_pattern, destination_hostgroup, apply, multiplex)   (5, 1, '^BEGIN', 10, 1, 0),
  (6, 1, '^COMMIT', 10, 1, 0);
LOAD MYSQL QUERY RULES TO RUNTIME;SAVE MYSQL QUERY RULES TO DISK;

📌 Use to fine-tune routing (e.g., send analytics queries to a dedicated replica).

5. Monitoring & Auto-Recovery

ProxySQL can auto-shun failing servers and recover them. Optimize monitoring:

-- Faster failure detectionUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='2000' WHERE variable_name='mysql-monitor_connect_timeout';
UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='1000' WHERE variable_name='mysql-monitor_ping_interval';
-- Auto-recover shunned serversUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='3' WHERE variable_name='mysql-shun_on_failures';
UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='60' WHERE variable_name='mysql-shun_recovery_time_sec';
LOAD MYSQL VARIABLES TO RUNTIME;SAVE MYSQL VARIABLES TO DISK;
SELECT hostname, status, weight FROM runtime_mysql_servers;

6. Thread & Memory Optimization

Match ProxySQL’s resources to your hardware:

-- Scale threads (4-8 per CPU core)UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='8' WHERE variable_name='mysql-threads';
-- Adjust stack size (1MB per thread)UPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='1048576' WHERE variable_name='mysql-stacksize';
-- Reduce overhead for high throughputUPDATE global_variables SET variable_value='false' WHERE variable_name='mysql-query_digests_lowercase';
LOAD MYSQL VARIABLES TO RUNTIME;SAVE MYSQL VARIABLES TO DISK;
  • Monitor CPU usage (top -H). If ProxySQL is bottlenecked, .
  • If memory is high, .

7. Final Checklist for Peak Performance

✅  — Optimize slow queries at the DB level.✅  — Reduce MySQL load.✅  — For read-heavy apps.✅ Set up read/write splitting — Balance load.✅  — Faster failover.✅  — Match CPU cores.

ProxySQL is a for database performance, but . By following these personalized optimizations, you can:

🚀 by 50%+ with smart pooling & caching.⚡ with intelligent routing.🛡️ with auto-recovery.

Now go benchmark, tweak, and watch your databases fly! 🚀

items.title: Automating NFS Server Setup with a User Data Script
items.author: Manohar Shetty
items.content:
14.Kubernetes Cluster Creation using Ansible & Terraform: NFS User Script

When setting up an NFS server on AWS, automating user creation, SSH key configuration, and package installation can save time and reduce errors. A user data script can be used to achieve this during instance initialization.

User data is a script that runs automatically when an EC2 instance boots up for the first time. It is useful for configuring instances, installing software, and setting up users.

User Data Script for NFS Server

The following script automates the setup of an NFS server by performing the following steps:

  • Creates a user named ansible-user
  • Configures SSH access for the user
  • Retrieves an SSH public key from an S3 bucket
  • Grants sudo privileges to the new user
#!/bin/bashsleep 60user_name="ansible-user"
user_home="/home/$user_name"
user_ssh_dir="$user_home/.ssh"
ssh_key_path="$user_ssh_dir/authorized_keys"
# Check if the user already exists
if id "$user_name" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "User $user_name already exists."
    exit 1sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" "$user_name"
# Inform user creation success
echo "User $user_name has been created successfully."
# Create .ssh directory if not exists
mkdir -p $user_ssh_dirapt-get update -y
apt-get install -y awscli
# Fetch and copy SSH public key from S3
aws s3 cp s3://my-key/server.pub $ssh_key_pathchown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home
# Add user to sudoers group
echo "ansible-user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/ansible-user
# Navigate to home directory and log a message
cd $user_home && echo "correct till this step" >>nfs.log 2>&1

Step-by-Step Breakdown of the Script

1. Setting Up the Script Header

  • This specifies that the script should run in a Bash shell.
  • sudo su - elevates the script to root privileges.
  • sleep 60 introduces a delay of 60 seconds, ensuring that the instance completes initial setup before executing further commands.
user_name="ansible-user"user_home="/home/$user_name"
user_ssh_dir="$user_home/.ssh"
ssh_key_path="$user_ssh_dir/authorized_keys"
  • These variables store paths related to the new user ansible-user and their SSH configuration.

3. Checking If the User Already Exists

if id "$username" &>/dev/null; then    echo "User $user_name already exists."fi
  • The id command checks if the user exists.
  • If the user is found, the script prints a message and exits.
if id "$user_name" &>/dev/null; then
sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" "$user_name"
  • adduser creates ansible-user without a password.
  • --gecos "" ensures no additional prompts for user details.
echo "User $user_name has been created successfully."sleep 20
  • A message is printed, and a 20-seconds pause is introduced.
mkdir -p $user_ssh_dirchmod 700 $user_ssh_dir
  • Creates the .ssh directory.
  • chmod 700 ensures that only the user has access.
apt-get update -yapt-get install -y awscli
  • Updates package lists and installs the AWS CLI to fetch SSH keys from S3.

7. Fetching and Configuring SSH Keys

aws s3 cp s3://my-key/server.pub $ssh_key_pathchmod 600 $ssh_key_path
chown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home
  • Downloads the public SSH key from an (my-key/server.pub).
  • chmod 600 ensures that only the owner can read the key.
  • chown -R sets ansible-user as the owner of the home directory.
echo "ansible-user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/ansible-user
  • Adds ansible-user to sudoers with .
cd $user_home && echo "correct till this step" >>nfs.log 2>&1
  • Moves to the user’s home directory.
  • Logs a success message to nfs.log.
  1. as userdata.sh.
  2. and paste the script into the  section.
  3. allow access to the S3 bucket.
  4. after the instance is running:
cat /home/ansible-user/nfs.log
  1. using the private key.

Automating the setup of an on AWS using a simplifies instance provisioning, reduces manual configuration, and ensures consistency. This script efficiently:

✅ Creates an ansible-user for secure access ✅ Sets up SSH authentication using an ✅ Installs for remote management ✅ Grants access for automation flexibility

By leveraging , you can ensure that every new instance is configured correctly from the moment it boots up, reducing human errors and improving deployment efficiency.

items.title: Automating AZURE Infrastructure with Terraform
items.author: Sai Charan
items.content:
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is a game-changer for cloud deployments, allowing automation, scalability, and repeatability. In this article, we will walk through a Terraform configuration for deploying an Azure environment. This setup includes a Virtual Network (VNet), subnets, Network Security Groups (NSGs), NAT Gateway, route tables, public IPs, and two Linux Virtual Machines.

The following Terraform code is structured to create

  • A with public and private subnets
  • Public and private route tables for network traffic routing
  • Network Security Groups (NSGs) to manage inbound and outbound traffic
  • for outbound internet access from private subnets
  • for specific machines
  • Linux Virtual Machines (VMs) application machine and tools machine
## Terraform Configuration for Azure Infrastructureresource "azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association" "nat-pub-ip-association" {
  nat_gateway_id       = azurerm_nat_gateway.nat_gateway.id
  public_ip_address_id = azurerm_public_ip.nat-pub-ip.id
resource "azurerm_public_ip" "nat-pub-ip" {
  name                = "nat_pub_ip"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  allocation_method   = "Static"
    environment = "Production"}
# Public IP for App Machine
resource "azurerm_public_ip" "app-machine-ip" {
  name                = "app-machine-ip"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  allocation_method   = "Static"    environment = "Production"}
# Public IP for Tools Machine
resource "azurerm_public_ip" "tools-machine-ip" {
  name                = "tools-machine-ip"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  allocation_method   = "Static"    environment = "Production"}
resource "azurerm_subnet_nat_gateway_association" "nat-subnet-association" {
  subnet_id      = azurerm_subnet.private-subnet.id
  nat_gateway_id = azurerm_nat_gateway.nat_gateway.id
}
resource "azurerm_nat_gateway" "nat_gateway" {
  name                    = "nat-gateway"
  location                = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name     = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  sku_name                = "Standard"
  idle_timeout_in_minutes = 10resource "azurerm_network_interface" "app-machine-nic" {
  name                = "app-machine-nic"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
    name                          = "internal"
    subnet_id                     = azurerm_subnet.public-subnet.id
    private_ip_address_allocation = "Dynamic"
    public_ip_address_id          = azurerm_public_ip.app-machine-ip.id 
  }resource "azurerm_network_interface" "tools-machine-nic" {
  name                = "tools-machine-nic"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
    name                          = "internal"
    subnet_id                     = azurerm_subnet.public-subnet.id
    private_ip_address_allocation = "Dynamic"
    public_ip_address_id          = azurerm_public_ip.tools-machine-ip.id 
  }
# Associate NSG with App Machine NIC
resource "azurerm_network_interface_security_group_association" "app-machine-nsg-association" {
  network_interface_id      = azurerm_network_interface.app-machine-nic.id
  network_security_group_id = azurerm_network_security_group.nsg.id
# Associate NSG with Tools Machine NIC
resource "azurerm_network_interface_security_group_association" "tools-machine-nsg-association" {
  network_interface_id      = azurerm_network_interface.tools-machine-nic.id
  network_security_group_id = azurerm_network_security_group.nsg.id
resource "azurerm_network_security_group" "nsg" {
  name                = "nsg"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name    name                       = "nsg-rule1"
    priority                   = 100
    direction                  = "Inbound"
    access                     = "Allow"
    protocol                   = "Tcp"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "22"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"   
    destination_address_prefix = "*"    name                       = "nsg-rule2"
    priority                   = 110
    direction                  = "Inbound"
    access                     = "Allow"
    protocol                   = "Tcp"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "80"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"   
    destination_address_prefix = "*"    name                       = "nsg-rule"
    priority                   = 100
    direction                  = "Outbound"
    access                     = "Allow"
    protocol                   = "Tcp"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "*"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"   
    destination_address_prefix = "*"    environment = "Production"}  required_providers {      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"    }}  features {}
  subscription_id = "3da7f2c9-7a36-46c4-9c93-bffa43a472a1"
}
resource "azurerm_resource_group" "capstone-project" {
  name     = "capstone_project"}resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "capstone-vnet" {
  name                = "capstone_vnet"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]    environment = "Production"}resource "azurerm_route_table" "public_route_table" {
  name                = "public-route-table"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
    name           = "public_route"
    address_prefix = "0.0.0.0/0"
    next_hop_type  = "Internet"    environment = "Production"}
resource "azurerm_route_table" "private_route_table" {
  name                = "private-route-table"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name    name           = "private_route"
    address_prefix = "0.0.0.0/0"
    next_hop_type  = "Internet"    environment = "Production"}resource "azurerm_subnet" "public-subnet" {
  name                 = "public_subnet"
  resource_group_name  = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.capstone-vnet.name
  address_prefixes     = ["10.0.1.0/24"]
resource "azurerm_subnet" "private-subnet" {
  name                 = "private_subnet"
  resource_group_name  = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.capstone-vnet.name
  address_prefixes     = ["10.0.2.0/24"]
}resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine" "app-machine" {
  name                  = "app-machine"
  location              = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name   = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  size                  = "Standard_B4ms"
  admin_username        = "azureuser"
  network_interface_ids = [azurerm_network_interface.app-machine-nic.id]    caching              = "ReadWrite"
    storage_account_type = "Standard_LRS"
  }    publisher = "Canonical"
    offer     = "0001-com-ubuntu-server-jammy"
    sku       = "22_04-lts-gen2"  }    username   = "azureuser"
    public_key = file("/home/sreemusiri/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  }
resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine" "tools-machine" {
  name                  = "tools-machine"
  location              = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.location
  resource_group_name   = azurerm_resource_group.capstone-project.name
  size               = "Standard_B4ms"
  admin_username        = "azureuser"
  network_interface_ids = [azurerm_network_interface.tools-machine-nic.id]
    caching              = "ReadWrite"
    storage_account_type = "Standard_LRS"
  }    publisher = "Canonical"
    offer     = "0001-com-ubuntu-server-jammy"
    sku       = "22_04-lts-gen2"  }    username   = "azureuser"
    public_key = file("/home/sreemusiri/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  }    environment = "staging"}

items.title: Devops CI: Continuous Integration in DevOps
items.author: Sourojit Das
items.content:
Continuous integration (CI) is an essential component of DevOps pipelines and agile development workflows. The purpose of this technique is to minimise the number and complexity of merge conflicts by introducing frequent mainline pushes. Once code has been integrated and tested, it is updated on local machines with frequent pulls so that developers are constantly working with the most recent version of the code.

In Traditional Integration methods, each developer receives a copy of the code from the central repository, starts from the same spot and continues working. They work as a team or individually and modify classes, methods, functions, etc to finish the task at hand. However, since all of them work without a clear picture of what the others are doing this leads to a change in the context in which the code is being written.

The issues from this opacity only increase with the size of the team and the amount of time passed since a developer got the most recent copy of the codebase from the repository.

It is to overcome these challenges that Continuous Integration came into being.

Defining the term: DevOps CI

Continuous integration (CI) is a pillar of the pipelines and an integral component of agile development workflows. This strategy aims to reduce the number and complexity of merge conflicts by performing frequent mainline pushes. Once code has been merged and tested, it is frequently pulled to local workstations, so developers are constantly working with the most recent version.

CI focuses on the build and integration phases of the conventional software development lifecycle. The CI pipeline is realized by combining automation (tools and services) with cultural adjustments (aligning team values and goals to DevOps principles).

In conventional software development methodologies, such as the waterfall model, features would be assigned to developers to build throughout the sprint. At the conclusion of the sprint, the engineers integrate their completed code to produce the application’s final codebase.

This strategy seems perfect, but in practice, two developers may find themselves modifying the same section of a file.

What happens if Developer A extends the functionality of a code snippet to accommodate their feature, but Developer B deletes the identical code snippet? Both have been using distinct versions of the same file for weeks, and now their modifications fundamentally disagree. This conflict resolution procedure is laborious and time-consuming.

It is clearly visible how this hazard came to be known as “merge hell” or “integration hell” when multiplied by the number of engineers working on hundreds or thousands of files for enterprise-scale applications.

CI solves this inefficiency in traditional development pipelines by increasing the merging and integration frequency from weeks to days or even hours via automation. Now, merge conflicts are significantly smaller and simpler in scope, accelerating the development team’s ability to resolve them. In addition, developers are now using a codebase that mirrors the changes made by their peers, ensuring that everyone is on the same page.

Apart from solving this issue, Continuous Integration has a number of other benefits to enterprises.

Benefits of CI to Enterprise

Continuous Integration in DevOps has several advantages for Enterprise organizations. Some of them are:

Every commit triggers automatic code review and testing. If the code fails, developers are told promptly to fix it. Developers now regularly validate their work instead of waiting until the conclusion of the development phase since CI encourages daily commits. Automated test feedback gives development teams more trust in the product. Since they tested each new feature or component against project criteria, the final product is more likely to meet them.

Tools that construct CI pipelines can also monitor the generation of features and other development objectives. This information is also utilized to keep other stakeholders aware of the deployment schedule.

The operations team can track when they can anticipate receiving release packages from development. Business teams may monitor the estimated delivery date for new features so they can keep their customers informed, and marketing teams can design marketing collateral and campaigns for the product’s new capabilities as they are being developed rather than after release.

As changes are made, code is continuously integrated and tested against test cases. This guarantees that the quality assurance (QA) team always has a tested build of the application to examine for performance and configuration requirements.

Instead of detecting defects after the coding process is complete, the QA team can convey them to the development team, hence accelerating the turnaround time.

The full benefits of CI can only be realized with a solid testing approach. Otherwise, a development team may only be able to streamline the process of committing faulty code.

This emphasis on testing provides additional incentive for business and development teams to properly identify objectives at the beginning of the life cycle. It enables the engineers to develop the necessary test cases to confirm that the code satisfies these objectives, for which careful planning is required.

The first step that many development teams take to accelerate the software lifecycle is continuous integration. Continuous delivery is the subsequent stage, which automates the release of the merged code for manual deployment.

Some organizations augment this methodology with continuous deployment, which automates the release and deployment processes such that the code enters production without the intervention of a person. These strategies collectively constitute the CI/CD pipeline.

Now that an understanding of Continuous Integration and its importance has been established, it is crucial to understand how it works.

CI enable software developers to divide responsibilities and code features separately in parallel. A few distinct tool sets are necessary to develop a CI pipeline that successfully supports this process. To track and integrate changes, a development team must first implement a version control system.

  1. The Developer checks out code to their workstation for editing.
  2. The developer commits modifications to the repository.
  3. Prior to accepting a commit, the code’s quality and syntax are examined.
  4. Commit initiates a build of the application.
  5. If the application cannot be constructed, the team is notified to address the problem.
  6. The build server executes unit tests on an application.
  7. If the application passes the test cases, the team is notified that the code can be merged.
  8. If the program fails test cases, the code is returned to the development team for bug fixes.
  9. This cycle should be repeated throughout the development phase.

A code repository is then required by a CI pipeline to hold the codebase. Git and GitHub are a popular version control system and repository combo. Finally, a testing suite is required for the pipeline. This subset of tools can be used to do code quality checks and syntax reviews before committing work, as well as unit testing after the codebase has been compiled and the application has been constructed.

Once the pipeline has been choreographed with the appropriate tools, the following would be an example of a CI workflow:

Performing CI with Jenkins

is an open-source, Java-based automation tool that may be customised with CI-specific plugins. Jenkins is a continuous integration and delivery solution that streamlines the software development and testing processes through automation.

It is a continuous integration and delivery system that automates the process of building and testing software, making it simpler for both developers to incorporate changes and end users to receive the latest version. It also integrates with a wide variety of testing and deployment tools, so the product can be distributed continually.

Incorporating Jenkins into enterprise processes allows the automation of software development processes and releases products faster. Jenkins unifies several steps in the software development life cycle, such as compilation, documentation, testing, packaging, staging, deployment, and static analysis.

It may also be used with a broad range of testing and deployment tools, allowing for consistent product dissemination. Automation of the software development process and expedited product releases are both possible with Jenkins’ addition to the DevOps infrastructure. Jenkins streamlines several phases of the SDLC into one, including coding, testing, packaging, staging, deployment, and static analysis.

For the purposes of this article a simple example is considered. A company has obtained a project with four modules. These have been allocated to two developers. A Continuous Delivery pipeline has been implemented to maintain consistency in development, where a single job will have two modules. These jobs are synchronized in a pipeline, and the build is checked at regular intervals. Jenkins was provided the project path from GitHub, and a build trigger option was set up after fixed time intervals through Jenkins.

Open Jenkins on the designated port

Create a new job with a new name

Pull the Source Code and proceed to build.

The second job can be created similarly.

The build trigger is then set up as below

Clicking on Build Now will set up the build job

Enterprises should consider leveraging testing platforms like BrowserStack Automate , which easily integrate into developers’ CI/CD pipelines to enable agile teams to scale up, to get the most out of a CI/CD strategy.

At this point, implementing a CI/CD pipeline is non-negotiable. CI/CD provides tools and procedures that benefit developers in order to adopt discipline, efficiency, and high levels of quality control in software development. A careful combination of human and automated tests can be used to build a CI/CD pipeline that will speed up development cycles and give better user experiences.

items.title: Automating AWS EC2 Instance Management with Ansible: A Real-Time Project
items.author: Sai Charan
items.content:
Infrastructure automation is a crucial skill in DevOps, and Ansible simplifies the process of provisioning and managing AWS EC2 instances. In this project, we will:

Create EC2 instances using Ansible loopsSet up passwordless authenticationAutomate the shutdown of Ubuntu instances using conditionals

Let’s dive into the implementation step by step.

Before starting, ensure you have the following:You have on your control node.AWS CLI is with IAM credentials. The amazon.aws collection is (ansible-galaxy collection install amazon.aws). Your AWS key pair is available for SSH access.

Task 1: Creating EC2 Instances using Ansible Loops

We will create:Two Ubuntu-based instancesOne CentOS-based instance

---- hosts: localhost  vars_files:    - name: Create EC2 instances        name: "{{ item.name }}"        instance_type: t3a.medium        region: us-east-1
        aws_access_key: "{{ ec2_access_key }}"  # From vault as defined
        aws_secret_key: "{{ ec2_secret_key }}"  # From vault as defined      
        subnet_id: subnet-0bf81627d238b09d9
        image_id: "{{ item.image }}"          environment: "{{ item.name }}"        - { image: "ami-0e2c8caa4b6378d8c", name: "manage-node-1" } # Update AMI ID 
        - { image: "ami-0453ec754f44f9a4a", name: "manage-node-2" } # based on region
        - { image: "ami-0e2c8caa4b6378d8c", name: "manage-node-3" }

Run the Playbook to Create EC2 Instances

ansible-playbook create_instances.yaml

Use the amazon.aws.ec2_instance module. Fetches AWS credentials securely from . Implements a to create multiple instances dynamically.

Task 2: Configuring Passwordless Authentication

To enable passwordless authentication between the Ansible control node and the EC2 instances

on the control node

ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048 -f ~/.ssh/ansible-key

to all managed instances using Ansible:

---- hosts: all    - name: Copy SSH public key to instances
      ansible.builtin.authorized_key:
        user: ubuntu        key: "{{ lookup('file', '~/.ssh/ansible-key.pub') }}"

Run the Playbook to Set Up Passwordless SSH Authentication

ansible-playbook setup_ssh.yaml

Verify connectivity using:

Task 3: Automating Shutdown for Ubuntu Instances Only

We will use to shut down only Ubuntu-based instances.

Playbook: shutdown_ubuntu.yaml

---- hosts: all    - name: Shutdown Ubuntu instances only
      ansible.builtin.command: /sbin/shutdown -t now
      when: ansible_facts['os_family'] == "Debian"

Run the Playbook Shutdown Ubuntu Instances Only

ansible-playbook shutdown_ubuntu.yaml

With these , we successfully: Automated EC2 provisioning with Ansible loops. Configured SSH passwordless authentication. Applied condition-based shutdown for Ubuntu instances.

items.title: Constructing Tickets with OS Ticket
items.author: Trevor Boodram
items.content:
Hello and welcome back in today's documentation before I went over on constructing OS ticket by running azure virtual machines. Next we will go over to create tickets to act like the users and assign it within our teams and we will also set up different service level agreements, to show the different severity of the ticket.

This part is now our agent panel and we can see the different access and we are in the dashboard of OS ticket website.

We can start off creating a New Department for System admin for parent support I choose Top Department and left all configurations to default.

We can now see that our department has now been created.

I also created teams to have different teams for different departments.

Next we want to create agents one for system admin and another for support.We can see that our agents have been created.

Then we can start to add a users and this is where the person will be ale to create the tickets.

We need to add an email for the user so we know how to contact them in the future if they are not available by phone.

Our User has been created successfully.

Then we can start to configure SLA agreements.As tickets come in SLA will determine the severity of the ticket of how important it is to handle.

I labeled this one Sev- A as most important which means it need to be handle right away within an hour.

This SLA is a little less severe but still as important to be looked at and resolve.

For Sev-C I set it to business hours so if a ticket has been created it will be shown during business hours.We can now see that our SLA have been completed.

Next we move on to create Topics this will be different categories so users can choose which most resembles there problem.

Created a Business Critical Outage and put it as Report a Problem.

Next I created another topic for Computer Issues and left it as report a problem.

Created another one for Equipment Requests and left it as General Inquiry.

Also created another topic for Password resets.

Then I also added for any other questions users might have where they might want general inquiry.

All of our topics have been created and added.

To first start creating tickets we sign in as a user this is where our users can start creating tickets.

Next I created a ticket for a user called Sam and he is reporting a problem in his department stating that they cannot access the sales protal.

Next we can sign in as the system administrator and we can see as the system admin that the ticket has been created from our user.

So Jane who is the system administrator took care of the issue an replied back to Sam stating that the issue has been resolved.You can experiment more by creating more tickets and answering them as well.

Thankyou for taking your time to read through my documentation of how it is like in the IT environment by having our users create tickets and our IT team answering those tickets.Ticketing systems can can be recorded and be prioritize, making it easier to track and manage tickets.Tickets can be routed to the right people to solve issues quickly and effectively.

items.title: Building a Lambda-Based Monitoring Stack with CloudWatch and SNS Alerts
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
Monitoring and observability are critical components of any serverless architecture. When dealing with AWS Lambda functions, understanding both infrastructure-level failures (such as timeouts or out-of-memory errors) and application-level issues (such as business logic failures) becomes essential. Relying solely on built-in metrics may not provide the visibility necessary for troubleshooting or alerting. Therefore, configuring enhanced monitoring, custom metrics, and automated alerts empowers teams to respond proactively to issues, maintain SLAs, and ensure system reliability.

In this tutorial, you will learn how to:

  • Deploy a Python-based Lambda function that simulates different failure scenarios.
  • Capture application-level errors using CloudWatch metric filters.
  • Trigger alerts using Amazon SNS.
  • Visualize metrics in a CloudWatch dashboard.

Step 1: Create the Lambda Function

  • Name: SimulatedErrorFunction
  • Role: Create a new role with basic Lambda permissions

Click to continue.

Replace the default code in the inline editor with the following:

import timeimport json
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    expected_response = event.get("expected_response", "success")
    wait_time = event.get("wait_time", 3)
        time.sleep(wait_time)
        if expected_response == "error":
            response = {                "body": {
                    "message": "Forced error triggered by input parameter.",
                    "request_id": context.aws_request_id            }
            logger.info(json.dumps(response))  # Structured log
            # Return body as JSON string per Lambda expectations
            response["body"] = json.dumps(response["body"])
            return response            "statusCode": 200,                "message": "Function executed successfully.",
                "request_id": context.aws_request_id
            }        logger.info(json.dumps(response))  # Structured log
        response["body"] = json.dumps(response["body"])
        return response        response = {            "body": {
                "message": "Internal server error.",
                "error": str(e),
                "request_id": context.aws_request_id
            }        logger.error(json.dumps(response))  # Structured log
        response["body"] = json.dumps(response["body"])
        return response

Click to save changes.

Navigate to Configuration > General configuration, click , set the timeout to 1 second, and then click .

Step 2: Create Lambda Test Events

Click in the Lambda console, define a new test event named SuccessEvent:

{  "expected_response": "success",}

Run the test. It should return HTTP status code 200.

Create another test event named ClientErrorEvent:

{  "expected_response": "error",}

Run the test. It should return HTTP status code 400.

Create a final test event named TimeoutErrorEvent:

{  "expected_response": "success",}

Run the test. The function should timeout, returning an infrastructure error.

After running at least one test, a CloudWatch Log Group will be automatically created. This will be necessary for the next step.

Step 3: Monitor Application-Level Errors

Trigger the function using the ClientErrorEvent multiple times to ensure logs with status 400 are recorded.

Click Actions > Create metric filter and use the following pattern:

{ $.statusCode = %4[0-9]{2}% }

Use the feature to validate matches. Proceed to define a new metric:

    Complete the wizard to create the filter.

    Step 4: Set Up an SNS Topic

    Navigate to the Amazon SNS Console, click , select , and name it LambdaErrorAlerts. Click .

    Click into the topic, go to , then click :

    • Endpoint: your email address

    Confirm the subscription from your inbox.

    Step 5: Create CloudWatch Alarms

    • Lambda > By Function Name > SimulatedErrorFunction > Errors
    • Threshold: >= 1 for 1 datapoint
    • Notification: Send to LambdaErrorAlerts

    Name the alarm LambdaErrorAlert and create it.

    Alarm for Application Errors

    Create another alarm using the custom metric:

    • Custom > Lambda > ClientError
    • Threshold: >= 1 for 1 datapoint
    • Notification: Send to LambdaErrorAlerts

    Name the alarm ClientErrorAlarm.

    This setup enables tracking both infrastructure and business logic failures.

    Step 6: Build CloudWatch Dashboard

    Navigate to Dashboards > Create dashboard, and provide the name LambdaMonitoringDashboard.

    Design the dashboard to highlight both infrastructure and application-level performance. Prioritize visual clarity and choose widgets that best convey the metrics most critical to your workload, such as Invocations, Errors, Duration, and the custom metric ClientError.

    Explore and utilize the different widget types available:

      Create a Custom Metric Widget (Error Percentage Gauge)

      To add a widget that displays the current error rate percentage, follow this procedure:

      In your dashboard, click the symbol and choose . This will add a widget you can later customize.

      Click , then navigate to the tab, and replace the widget source configuration with the following:

      {    "metrics": [
              [ "Custom/Lambda", "ClientError", { "id": "m1", "region": "us-east-1", "visible": false } ],
              [ "AWS/Lambda", "Errors", "FunctionName", "SimulatedErrorFunction", { "id": "m2", "region": "us-east-1", "visible": false } ],
              [ ".", "Invocations", ".", ".", { "id": "m3", "region": "us-east-1", "visible": false } ],
              [ { "expression": "(m2 + m1) / m3 * 100", "label": "ErrorRate (%)", "id": "errorRate", "region": "us-east-1", "color": "#d62728" } ]    "view": "gauge",    "title": "Lambda Error Rate (%) — Current Time Range",        "left": {            "max": 100,        }    "setPeriodToTimeRange": true,    "stat": "Sum",}

      This widget configuration combines both infrastructure-level (Errors) and application-level (ClientError) failures, divides them by total Invocations, and displays the result as a percentage. It provides an at-a-glance view of your Lambda function's current error rate.

      Click , then click .

      If you’ve named resources differently from this tutorial, make sure to adapt the metric names and dimensions accordingly.
      It may take a few minutes for new log data to be processed and reflected in the dashboard widget. You can trigger additional requests using your test events in Lambda to generate fresh log entries and verify the visualization updates correctly.

      Once you’ve added all desired widgets, click to persist your configuration. You now have a customized dashboard that provides actionable visibility into both operational and application-level Lambda performance metrics.

      By following this tutorial, you’ve built a monitoring stack that combines AWS Lambda, CloudWatch Logs, Metric Filters, Alarms, and SNS. This approach enables both operational and application-level visibility into your serverless workloads, enhancing incident response and observability practices.

      If you found this guide helpful, stay connected for more insights on AI, cloud security, and AWS automation:

items.title: Accelerating CI with AWS CodeBuild: Parallel test execution now available
items.author: Sébastien Stormacq
items.content:
I’m excited to announce that AWS CodeBuild now supports parallel test execution, so you can run your test suites concurrently and reduce build times significantly.

Sequential execution of the test suite

Parallel execution of the test suite

Very long test times pose a significant challenge when running continuous integration (CI) at scale. As projects grow in complexity and team size, the time required to execute comprehensive test suites can increase dramatically, leading to extended pipeline execution times. This not only delays the delivery of new features and bug fixes, but also hampers developer productivity by forcing them to wait for build results before proceeding with their tasks. I have experienced pipelines that took up to 60 minutes to run, only to fail at the last step, requiring a complete rerun and further delays. These lengthy cycles can erode developer trust in the CI process, contribute to frustration, and ultimately slow down the entire software delivery cycle. Moreover, long-running tests can lead to resource contention, increased costs because of wasted computing power, and reduced overall efficiency of the development process.

With parallel test execution in CodeBuild, you can now run your tests concurrently across multiple build compute environments. This feature implements a sharding approach where each build node independently executes a subset of your test suite. CodeBuild provides environment variables that identify the current node number and the total number of nodes, which are used to determine which tests each node should run. There is no control build node or coordination between nodes at build time—each node operates independently to execute its assigned portion of your tests.

To enable test splitting, configure the batch fanout section in your , specifying the desired parallelism level and other relevant parameters. Additionally, use the codebuild-tests-run utility in your build step, along with the appropriate test commands and the chosen splitting method.

The tests are split based on the sharding strategy you specify. offers two sharding strategies:

  • This strategy sorts test files alphabetically and distributes them in chunks equally across parallel test environments. Changes in the names or quantity of test files might reassign files across shards.
  • This strategy fixes the distribution of tests across shards by using a consistent hashing algorithm. It maintains existing file-to-shard assignments when new files are added or removed.

CodeBuild supports automatic merging of test reports when running tests in parallel. With automatic test report merging, CodeBuild consolidates tests reports into a single test summary, simplifying result analysis. The merged report includes aggregated pass/fail statuses, test durations, and failure details, reducing the need for manual report processing. You can view the merged results in the CodeBuild console, retrieve them using the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI), or integrate them with other reporting tools to streamline test analysis.

Let me demonstrate how to implement parallel testing in a project. For this demo, I created a very basic Python project with hundreds of tests. To speed things up, I asked Amazon Q Developer on the command line to create a project and 1,800 test cases. Each test case is in a separate file and takes one second to complete. Running all tests in a sequence requires 30 minutes, excluding the time to provision the environment.

In this demo, I run the test suite on ten compute environments in parallel and measure how long it takes to run the suite.

To do so, I added a file to my project.

version: 0.2

batch:
  fast-fail: false
  build-fanout:
    parallelism: 10 # ten runtime environments 
    ignore-failure: false

phases:
  install:
    commands:
      - echo 'Installing Python dependencies'
      - dnf install -y python3 python3-pip
      - pip3 install --upgrade pip
      - pip3 install pytest
  build:
    commands:
      - echo 'Running Python Tests'
      - |
         codebuild-tests-run \
          --test-command 'python -m pytest --junitxml=report/test_report.xml' \
          --files-search "codebuild-glob-search 'tests/test_*.py'" \
          --sharding-strategy 'equal-distribution'
  post_build:
    commands:
      - echo "Test execution completed"

reports:
  pytest_reports:
    files:
      - "*.xml"
    base-directory: "report"
    file-format: JUNITXML

There are three parts to highlight in the YAML file.

First, there’s a section under . The command tells CodeBuild how many test environments to run in parallel. The command indicates if failure in any of the fanout build tasks can be ignored.

Second, I use the pre-installed command to run my tests.

This command receives the complete list of test files and decides which of the tests must be run on the current node.

  • Use the argument to choose between equally distributed or stable distribution, as I explained earlier.
  • Use the argument to pass all the files that are candidates for a run. We recommend to use the provided command for performance reasons, but any file search tool, such as find(1), will work.
  • I pass the actual test command to run on the shard with the argument.

Lastly, the section instructs CodeBuild to collect and merge the test reports on each node.

Now, I’m ready to trigger an execution of the test suite. I can commit new code on my GitHub repository or trigger the build in the console.

After a few minutes, I see a status report of the different steps of the build; with a status for each test environment or shard.

When the test is complete, I select the tab to access the merged test reports.

The section aggregates all test data from all shards and keeps the history for all builds. I select my most recent build in the section to access the detailed report.

As expected, I can see the aggregated and the individual status for each of my 1,800 test cases. In this demo, they’re all passing, and the report is green.

The 1,800 tests of the demo project take one second each to complete. When I run this test suite sequentially, it took 35 minutes to complete. When I run the test suite in parallel on ten compute environments, it took 6 minutes to complete, including the time to provision the environments. The parallel run took 17.9 percent of the time of the sequential run. Actual numbers will vary with your projects.

This new capability is compatible with all testing frameworks. The documentation includes examples for Django, Elixir, Go, Java (Maven), Javascript (Jest), Kotlin, PHPUnit, Pytest, Ruby (Cucumber), and Ruby (RSpec).

For test frameworks that don’t accept space-separated lists, the CLI provides a flexible alternative through the CODEBUILD_CURRENT_SHARD_FILES environment variable. This variable contains a newline-separated list of test file paths for the current build shard. You can use it to adapt to different test framework requirements and format test file names.

You can further customize how tests are split across environments by writing your own sharding script and using the CODEBUILD_BATCH_BUILD_IDENTIFIER environment variable, which is automatically set in each build. You can use this technique to implement framework-specific parallelization or optimization.

With parallel test execution, you can now complete your test suites in a fraction of the time previously required, accelerating your development cycle and improving your team’s productivity.

I invite you to try parallel test execution in CodeBuild today. Visit the AWS CodeBuild documentation to learn more and get started with parallelizing your tests.

— seb

PS: Here’s the prompt I used to create the demo application and its test suite: “I’m writing a blog post to announce codebuild parallel testing. Write a very simple python app that has hundreds of tests, each test in a separate test file. Each test takes one second to complete.”

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)
