items.title: Distributed Tracing with Jaeger: Debugging Microservices Like a Pro
items.author: NIKITA VERMA
items.content:
Microservices are awesome — until something goes wrong. With requests bouncing between dozens of services, finding the culprit behind a slow response or a failed transaction can feel like searching for a needle in a haystack. That’s where comes in, and Jaeger is here to save the day! 🌟

Why Distributed Tracing Matters

Think of distributed tracing as a GPS for your application’s requests. It tracks a request’s journey across services, giving you:

  • : Identify where latency or errors creep in.
  • : See the full path of a request, not just isolated logs.
  • : Spot bottlenecks and optimize accordingly.

If logs are the heartbeat of your app, tracing is the X-ray — showing you the hidden connections.

Enter Jaeger: The Open-Source Tracing Hero 🦸‍♂️

Jaeger, built by Uber and now part of the CNCF, is a lightweight, powerful tool for distributed tracing. It’s designed to handle the chaos of microservices with:

  • : Works seamlessly in Kubernetes and beyond.
  • : Integrates with popular frameworks like OpenTelemetry.
  • : Visualize traces with timelines and dependency graphs.

Jaeger doesn’t just track requests — it tells the story of how they unfold.

Setting Up Jaeger in Kubernetes 🚀

Ready to get tracing? Here’s how to deploy Jaeger in your cluster.

  • A running Kubernetes cluster.
  • Helm installed (because who doesn’t love Helm?).

1️⃣ Add the Jaeger Helm repository:

helm repo add jaegertracing https://jaegertracing.github.io/helm-charts helm repo update

2️⃣ Install Jaeger with its all-in-one deployment (perfect for testing):

helm install jaeger jaegertracing/jaeger --namespace tracing --create-namespace

Step 2: Instrument Your Application

Jaeger needs your app to send traces. Use OpenTelemetry (the industry standard) to instrument your code:

  • Add the OpenTelemetry SDK to your app (e.g., Python, Go, Node.js).
  • Configure it to export traces to Jaeger’s endpoint (http://jaeger:14268/api/traces).

Example for a Node.js app:

const { NodeTracerProvider } = require('@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node'); const { JaegerExporter } = require('@opentelemetry/exporter-jaeger'); const provider = new NodeTracerProvider(); const exporter = new JaegerExporter({ endpoint: 'http://jaeger:14268/api/traces' }); provider.register();

Step 3: Access the Jaeger UI

1️⃣ Port-forward to Jaeger’s query service:

kubectl port-forward svc/jaeger-query 16686:16686 -n tracing

Jaeger in Action: What You’ll See 📈

  • : A waterfall of spans showing how long each service took.
  • : Visualize which services talk to each other.
  • : Spot failed requests and drill into the details.

It’s like a detective’s corkboard, but for your microservices.

Fun Challenges for You! 🎮

Test your tracing skills:

  • Create a custom span to track a specific function in your app.
  • Simulate a slow service and use Jaeger to find the bottleneck.
  • Compare Jaeger’s dependency graph with your app’s architecture diagram — do they match?

Tips for Mastering Jaeger 🛠

  • : Avoid overwhelming Jaeger by sampling traces (e.g., 1% of requests).
  • : Enrich spans with tags like user IDs or request payloads.
  • : Combine tracing with logs for the ultimate debugging combo.

Jaeger turns microservices chaos into clarity. It’s the perfect companion to your Loki-Grafana setup, giving you logs traces in harmony. Next time, we’ll explore how to tie it all together with OpenTelemetry — the glue that unifies observability. Until then, happy tracing! 🔎

items.title: Kafka Broker Vs. Controller Rack Awareness — A Deep Dive Into KRaft Clusters
items.author: Noel
items.content:
Kafka Broker Vs. Controller Rack Awareness — A Deep Dive Into KRaft Clusters

Rack awareness is a critical feature in Apache Kafka that enhances fault tolerance, availability, and disaster recovery by distributing replicas across different racks or availability zones (AZs). By ensuring that partitions are not concentrated in a single rack, Kafka reduces the impact of hardware failures, network outages, or regional disruptions on the cluster’s availability.

Traditionally, rack awareness has been associated with Kafka brokers, influencing partition leader election and replica placement. However, in KRaft mode (Kafka Raft mode), where Kafka controllers replace ZooKeeper for metadata management, understanding the implications of rack awareness on controllers becomes crucial for ensuring durability and minimizing downtime.

Rack Awareness In KRaft Mode — Why It Matters?

KRaft eliminates ZooKeeper and introduces a quorum-based controller architecture where controllers manage metadata directly. This raises key architectural questions -

🔹 Should controllers be rack-aware in the same way brokers are?

🔹 How does rack awareness impact leader election, partition replication, and failover in KRaft?

🔹 Are there best practices for placing controllers in a multi-rack setup?

Key Questions Addressed in This Article

1️⃣ How does rack awareness impact Kafka leader election, partition replication, and failover?

2️⃣ What are the differences in rack awareness behavior between Kafka brokers and controllers?

3️⃣ How can organizations optimize KRaft clusters to ensure durability, consistency, and low-latency metadata management?

🚀 By the end of this article, you will have a deep understanding of rack awareness in KRaft and how we at Xinthe go about designing a fault-tolerant Kafka deployment that balances availability, latency, and resilience.

Understanding KRaft — A Broker-Only Kafka Architecture

  • What Is KRaft (Kafka Raft Mode)?

KRaft (Kafka Raft mode) is Kafka’s new mode of operation that eliminates the dependency on ZooKeeper and integrates the consensus mechanism directly into the Kafka cluster. This simplifies metadata management, improves scalability, and enhances failure recovery.

In pre-KRaft Kafka, ZooKeeper was responsible for -

🔹 (topic partitions, broker state, and controller election).

🔹 Coordinating leader election for partitions.

🔹 Handling broker failures and reassigning leadership.

With KRaft, Kafka introduces a built-in Raft-based consensus mechanism where dedicated Kafka controllers manage all metadata operations, enabling -

✅ Lower metadata replication latencies (avoiding ZooKeeper’s bottleneck).

✅ Faster leader elections with Raft’s quorum-based model.

✅ Increased scalability by removing ZooKeeper as a central point of coordination.

  • Key Components In A KRaft Cluster

Unlike ZooKeeper-based Kafka, where controllers were dynamically elected among brokers, KRaft separates controllers from brokers in a dedicated metadata plane.

🔹 Role Of Controllers In KRaft

✔ Maintain the metadata log for the cluster, including broker registrations, topic configurations, and partition assignments.

✔ Use the Raft consensus protocol to ensure metadata consistency across multiple controllers.

✔ Elect partition leaders and coordinate replica reassignments in the event of broker failures.

🔹 Role Of Brokers In KRaft

✔ Handle producer and consumer traffic, managing read and write requests.

✔ Store and replicate topic partitions, ensuring high availability of data.

✔ Follow controller instructions regarding partition leadership and reassignment.

📌 Key Difference From ZooKeeper Mode:

In pre-KRaft Kafka, controllers were brokers that took on metadata responsibilities. In KRaft, controllers are dedicated nodes, separate from brokers, removing metadata bottlenecks and enhancing fault tolerance.

  • Why This Matters For Rack Awareness?

Understanding the separation between controllers and brokers is critical when evaluating rack awareness strategies in KRaft mode -

✔ Rack awareness traditionally applies to brokers to ensure partition replicas are evenly distributed across racks.

✔ But should rack awareness also apply to controllers?

✔ How does rack placement impact metadata consistency, leader elections, and failure recovery?

Broker Rack Awareness — How Kafka Spreads Partition Replicas

  • What Is Broker Rack Awareness?

In Apache Kafka, broker rack awareness ensures that partition replicas are evenly distributed across different racks or availability zones (AZs). This helps in -

✅ Minimizing data loss risk in case of .

✅ Ensuring high availability for even when infrastructure failures occur.

✅ across geographically or physically distinct racks.

📌 Why Rack Awareness Is Important For Kafka Brokers

🔹 → Partition replicas could be randomly placed, potentially concentrating all replicas within a single rack. If that rack fails, the entire topic partition becomes unavailable.

🔹 → Kafka ensures replica diversity, so at least one replica is always available in another rack in case of failure.

  • How Broker Rack Awareness Works

1️⃣ Rack Metadata Configuration

Each Kafka broker is assigned a broker.rack property in the server.properties configuration file. This property informs Kafka of the broker’s physical location (rack or AZ).

📌 Example — Assigning Rack Metadata In server.properties

broker.id=1broker.rack=us-east-1a

📌 Example — Assigning Racks To Multiple Brokers

broker.id=2broker.rack=us-east-1b
broker.id=3broker.rack=us-east-1c

Kafka automatically uses these rack assignments to distribute partition replicas across racks.

2️⃣ Partition Replication Across Racks

Kafka ensures that partition replicas are spread across racks to prevent data loss from rack failures.

📌 Example — Replication Strategy For A Topic Partition

📌 Equivalent YAML Representation

Partition: topic-1Replicas:  - B2 (Rack R2)

✔ If Rack R1 fails, Kafka can promote a replica from R2 or R3 to maintain service availability.

✔ Kafka’s replica assignment algorithm ensures an even distribution of partitions across racks.

3️⃣ Leader & Follower Selection

Kafka ensures that at least one replica is placed in another rack, so if one rack fails, a leader is still available from another rack.

✅ Leader brokers handle producer and consumer requests.

✅ Follower brokers replicate data for failover.

📌 Example — Leader & Follower Distribution With Rack Awareness

✔ If B1 (Rack R1) fails, Kafka promotes B2 (Rack R2) as the new leader.

✔ If a consumer fetches data, Kafka ensures it can read from the nearest available broker for efficiency.

  • Benefits Of Broker Rack Awareness

✔ Prevents complete data loss due to rack failures.

✔ Ensures at least one replica remains available in another rack.

✅ 2. Improved Read Availability

✔ Consumers can still fetch data even if one rack is down.

✔ Ensures low-latency reads from multiple AZs or data centers.

✅ 3. Better Load Balancing

✔ Distributes traffic across racks for efficient resource utilization.

✔ Helps avoid bottlenecks in high-load Kafka clusters.

Controller Rack Awareness — A New Dimension In KRaft

  • Why Does Controller Rack Awareness Matter?

With KRaft (Kafka Raft mode), the ZooKeeper dependency is eliminated, and dedicated Kafka controllers manage all cluster metadata operations. This introduces a new dimension to rack awareness -

✔ Controllers are responsible for leader election, metadata propagation, and partition reassignment.

✔ If all controllers are in the same rack, a single rack failure could cripple the cluster, making metadata inaccessible.

✔ Spreading controllers across multiple racks enhances availability, resilience, and failover efficiency.

📌 Why Controller Rack Awareness Is Critical In KRaft?

In traditional ZooKeeper-based Kafka, rack awareness was only necessary for brokers. However, in KRaft mode, ensuring controller redundancy across racks is equally critical.

  • How Controller Rack Awareness Works?

1️⃣ Controller Election With Rack Distribution

In KRaft mode, Kafka controllers are separate from brokers and must be highly available. By configuring controller rack awareness, controllers can be distributed across multiple racks, preventing correlated failures.

✅ Ensure controllers are not concentrated in the same rack to prevent a single point of failure.

✅ Use the controller.rack setting to define which rack each controller belongs to.

📌 Example — Controller Rack Configuration In KRaft

node.id=1001process.roles=controller
controller.rack=us-east-1a
node.id=1002process.roles=controller
controller.rack=us-east-1b
node.id=1003process.roles=controller
controller.rack=us-east-1c

✔ Here, controllers are spread across three racks (us-east-1a, us-east-1b, us-east-1c).

✔ This prevents all controllers from being taken down if a single rack fails.

2️⃣ Failover Scenarios — Ensuring Controller High Availability

In KRaft mode, Kafka controllers operate using a Raft-based consensus mechanism, where one controller is elected as the active leader and others act as standby followers.

✔ If the active controller fails, a standby controller from another rack automatically takes over.

✔ This prevents metadata loss and ensures seamless leader election.

📌 Failover Process For Controllers Across Racks

📌 Example Of Failover Scenario

✔ This prevents single-rack failures from taking down the entire cluster.

✔ Metadata consistency is maintained, ensuring minimal impact on Kafka clients.

3️⃣ Metadata Propagation Across Controllers

Kafka controllers synchronize metadata across racks to ensure -

✔ High consistency between leader and standby controllers.

✔ Low-latency metadata propagation across large-scale deployments.

✔ Instant failover activation in case of a controller failure.

📌 How Metadata Propagation Works In KRaft?

1️⃣ Active controller (C1) receives metadata updates from brokers.

2️⃣ It replicates the metadata changes to standby controllers (C2, C3).

3️⃣ Standby controllers stay synchronized and ready to take over instantly if needed.

📌 Example — Metadata Replication Across Controllers

✔ This ensures that metadata is always available, even if the active controller fails.

  • Benefits Of Controller Rack Awareness

✅ 1. Prevents Single-Rack Controller Failures From Disrupting The Cluster

✔ By spreading controllers across racks, a single rack failure won’t result in total metadata loss.

✔ Ensures that leader election can still occur, keeping the cluster operational.

✅ 2. Ensures Fast Failover In Case Of Controller Loss

✔ If the active controller fails, a standby controller from another rack takes over instantly.

✔ This minimizes metadata downtime and prevents cluster unavailability.

✅ 3. Improves Cluster Metadata Consistency Across Availability Zones

✔ Controllers continuously synchronize metadata across racks, ensuring that failover is seamless.

✔ Brokers always have a functioning metadata source, even if a rack experiences failure.

Broker Vs. Controller Rack Awareness — Key Differences

  • Understanding Rack Awareness In Brokers Vs. Controllers

Rack awareness plays two distinct roles in Apache Kafka depending on whether it applies to brokers or controllers.

✅ It ensures partition replicas are distributed across multiple racks, preventing data loss in case of rack failures.

✅ It ensures that Kafka cluster metadata remains available even if a rack goes down, preventing full-cluster failures.

The table below summarizes the key differences between broker and controller rack awareness -

  • 📌 Broker Vs. Controller Rack Awareness — Key Differences
  • 1️⃣ Broker Rack Awareness — Preventing Partition Data Loss

In a Kafka broker setup, partition replicas are placed across different racks to avoid data loss.

📌 Example — Partition Replica Placement With Broker Rack Awareness

✔ If Rack R1 fails, Kafka automatically promotes Broker-2 (R2) as the new leader for topic-1.

📌 Why Is Broker Rack Awareness Important?

✅ Prevents total data loss if a rack fails.

✅ Ensures high availability by keeping replicas in different racks.

✅ Balances partitions across racks to avoid overload on a single rack.

  • 2️⃣ Controller Rack Awareness — Ensuring Cluster Metadata Availability

Unlike brokers, Kafka controllers in KRaft mode handle metadata management and leader election. If all controllers are in a single rack, a failure in that rack could cause a total cluster outage.

📌 Example — Controller Placement With Rack Awareness

✔ If C1 in Rack R1 fails, the standby controller in Rack R2 (C2) takes over immediately.

📌 Why Is Controller Rack Awareness Important?

✅ Prevents cluster-wide failures due to controller loss.

✅ Ensures fast leader election and metadata consistency.

✅ Spreads controllers across racks to minimize correlated failures.

  • 3️⃣ Key Takeaways — Broker Vs. Controller Rack Awareness

📌 Both broker and controller rack awareness should be implemented together for a highly available, fault-tolerant Kafka cluster.

Best Practices For Configuring Rack Awareness In KRaft

Rack awareness in KRaft (Kafka Raft mode) is critical for ensuring high availability, fault tolerance, and optimized partition replication. Implementing best practices for both brokers and controllers ensures a resilient Kafka cluster that can withstand rack failures.

  • 1️⃣ Defining Rack Metadata For Brokers & Controllers

Rack metadata allows Kafka to distribute brokers and controllers across different racks, ensuring fault tolerance and load balancing.

📌 Configuring Rack Awareness For Brokers

Each Kafka broker should be assigned to a specific rack using the broker.rack property.

📌 Example — Broker Rack Configuration (server.properties)

broker.id=1broker.rack=us-east-1a
broker.id=2broker.rack=us-east-1b
broker.id=3broker.rack=us-east-1c

✔ This ensures that partition replicas are spread across multiple racks, minimizing data loss risks.

📌 Configuring Rack Awareness For Controllers

Unlike brokers, Kafka controllers are responsible for metadata management and leader election. Assigning them to different racks ensures metadata availability in case of failures.

📌 Example — Controller Rack Configuration (kraft-controller.properties)

controller.id=1001controller.rack=us-east-1a
controller.id=1002controller.rack=us-east-1b
controller.id=1003controller.rack=us-east-1c

✔ At least one controller should be in a different rack from most brokers to prevent correlated failures.

  • 2️⃣ Spreading Controllers & Brokers Evenly Across Racks

📌 Key Best Practices For Rack Distribution

✅ Ensure at least one controller is in a different rack from most brokers to prevent metadata failures.

✅ Deploy a minimum of three controllers to maintain quorum-based leadership election.

✅ Distribute broker leaders evenly across racks to avoid overloading a single rack.

📌 Example — Recommended Rack Distribution

✔ If Rack R1 fails, metadata remains available via Controller-2 (R2) and Controller-3 (R3).

✔ Partitions continue serving traffic via available brokers in R2 and R3.

  • 3️⃣ Setting Proper Replication & Leadership Balance

📌 Using Rack-Aware Replica Selection

Kafka provides an option to optimize partition leadership election based on rack awareness. This ensures -

✔ Replica selection prefers leaders from different racks.

✔ Faster reads by reducing cross-rack network traffic.

✔ Improved failover performance in case of broker failures.

📌 Enable Rack-Aware Replica Selection (server.properties)

replica.selector.class=org.apache.kafka.common.replica.RackAwareReplicaSelector

✔ This ensures that Kafka intelligently picks leaders from different racks, reducing read latency.

📌 Ensuring Rack-Aware Leader Assignment

Kafka randomly assigns partition leaders, but this can lead to imbalanced workloads. Configuring rack-aware leader selection ensures -

✔ Leaders are evenly distributed across racks.

✔ Read latency is optimized by selecting leaders close to consumers.

📌 Enable Rack-Aware Leader Balancing

kafka-reassign-partitions.sh --zookeeper <zookeeper_host> --broker-rack-aware

✔ This command ensures that Kafka evenly distributes partition leaders across racks.

  • Key Takeaways — Implementing Rack Awareness In KRaft

✅ Assign brokers and controllers to racks using broker.rack and controller.rack.

✅ Ensure controllers are spread across racks to prevent metadata failures.

✅ Use replica.selector.class=RackAwareReplicaSelector for intelligent leader election.

✅ Distribute partition leaders evenly to avoid performance bottlenecks.

Performance & Failure Scenarios In Kafka Rack Awareness

Proper rack awareness configuration ensures fault tolerance, optimized leader election, and high availability in KRaft-based Kafka clusters. However, real-world failures and imbalanced cluster utilization can still impact performance.

This section explores how Kafka handles failures and potential risks when rack awareness is not configured correctly.

  • 1️⃣ Rack Failure Simulation — What Happens When A Rack Goes Down?

📌 Scenario — Rack us-east-1a Fails

Let’s assume a Kafka cluster is spread across three racks (us-east-1a, us-east-1b, and us-east-1c).

📌 Impact Of Rack Failure On Kafka Cluster

✔ Partition replicas in us-east-1b and us-east-1c remain available.

✔ A new controller (Controller-2 from us-east-1b) takes over leader election.

✔ Kafka automatically re-elects partition leaders among the remaining brokers.

✔ Clients experience minimal downtime as Kafka handles the failover transparently.

📌 Key Lessons From Rack Failures

✅ Always have at least one controller in a different rack than most brokers.

✅ Distribute partition replicas across multiple racks to prevent data loss.

✅ Monitor Kafka logs and cluster metrics to ensure fast failover in real-world failures.

  • 2️⃣ Unbalanced Rack Utilization — Hidden Performance Risks

📌 Issue — Most Brokers & Controllers Are In The Same Rack

If too many brokers and controllers are placed in a single rack, the risk of failure increases significantly.

Example of an imbalanced Kafka cluster -

❌ : If us-east-1a fails, both active and standby controllers are lost, crashing the cluster.

❌ : High traffic is concentrated on a single rack, overloading network bandwidth.

❌ : Leader elections take longer due to lack of controllers in other racks.

📌 Solution — Manually Balance Controllers & Brokers Across Racks

✔ Ensure that controllers are evenly distributed across racks to prevent correlated failures.

✔ Use a minimum of three controllers for fault tolerance.

✔ Reassign partition leaders evenly across brokers in different racks.

📌 Balanced Kafka Rack Distribution Example

✔ If Rack us-east-1a fails, controllers in us-east-1b and us-east-1c ensure cluster stability.

✔ Leader election remains fast, since controllers are distributed across different racks.

✔ Kafka remains operational with minimal downtime.

  • 📌 Final Takeaways — Ensuring Performance & Fault Tolerance

✅ Simulate failures to validate rack awareness effectiveness (e.g., temporarily shutting down brokers in a rack).

✅ Monitor Kafka leader distribution across racks to avoid overloaded brokers.

✅ Use Prometheus & Grafana for real-time failure detection in rack-aware Kafka clusters.

✅ Always distribute controllers and brokers across multiple racks to ensure high availability.

Future Of Rack Awareness In Kafka

As Kafka evolves, rack awareness is expected to become more automated, intelligent, and optimized for large-scale distributed deployments. With the maturity of KRaft, new features will enhance failover mechanisms, controller placement, and leader election while reducing manual configuration overhead.

This section explores the future direction of rack awareness in Kafka and how AI-driven optimizations will improve performance.

  • 1️⃣ Improved Automated Rack Awareness

Currently, Kafka administrators manually assign broker.rack and controller.rack configurations to ensure proper failover and partition distribution. However, future versions of Kafka may introduce -

📌 Dynamic Rack Awareness Without Manual Configuration

🚀 Potential Enhancements In Future Kafka Releases:

✔ : Kafka could dynamically assign brokers and controllers to racks based on cloud infrastructure metadata.

✔ Self-Healing Rack Placement: If a rack fails, Kafka could automatically reassign leaders and replicas to maintain redundancy.

✔ Rack-Aware Partition Rebalancing: Kafka could auto-rebalance partitions across racks without requiring kafka-reassign-partitions.sh.

📌 Example Of A Fully Automated Rack-Aware Configuration

Instead of manually defining rack metadata, Kafka could fetch the rack placement dynamically -

broker.id=1auto.rack.awareness=true

✔ Reduces admin workload and improves fault tolerance in multi-cloud and hybrid environments.

✔ Eliminates the risk of misconfigurations caused by manual rack assignments.

  • 2️⃣ AI-Driven Rack Optimization

Machine learning and AI-driven traffic analysis could optimize broker and controller placement dynamically.

📌 How AI Could Improve Rack Awareness

🔹 Real-Time Load Balancing: AI models analyze network traffic, partition leader distribution, and workload spikes to automatically adjust broker placements.

🔹 Predictive Failure Mitigation: AI models detect early signs of rack failures and proactively migrate partitions to healthier nodes.

🔹 Automated Resource Scaling: If a rack becomes overloaded, AI could recommend provisioning additional brokers or controllers in underutilized racks.

📌 Example — AI-Optimized Rack Placement

✔ Self-learning AI models could optimize Kafka’s performance dynamically.

✔ Reduces operational complexity in large-scale deployments.

  • 3️⃣ KRaft Maturity & Enhancements

KRaft (Kafka Raft mode) is still evolving, and as it matures, better tooling for rack-aware leader election and controller failover will emerge.

📌 Expected Enhancements In Future KRaft Releases

✅ Dynamic Controller Failover Improvements:

Future versions may auto-promote standby controllers in rack-aware order, prioritizing the most optimal controllers first.

✅ Optimized Rack-Aware Leader Election:

Kafka currently does not prioritize rack-aware leader selection beyond the replica selector class.

Future enhancements may introduce smarter leader election algorithms based on network latency and resource availability.

✅ Better Visualization & Monitoring Tools:

New KRaft-aware monitoring dashboards in Kafka Manager, Confluent Control Center, and Prometheus.

Real-time rack-aware replication heatmaps to identify imbalances and bottlenecks.

  • 📌 Final Takeaways — The Future Of Rack Awareness In Kafka

✔ Future Kafka versions may automate rack-aware failover, reducing manual admin effort.

✔ AI-driven optimization will dynamically place brokers and controllers based on traffic patterns and failure risks.

✔ As KRaft matures, new tools for rack-aware leader election and self-healing clusters will emerge.

Rack awareness is a critical factor in ensuring high availability, fault tolerance, and resilience in Apache Kafka clusters — especially in KRaft-based deployments where controllers and brokers play distinct roles. Proper rack-aware configurations prevent data loss, metadata inconsistencies, and cluster-wide failures.

For our clients running Kafka at scale, we at Xinthe resort to prioritizing both broker and controller rack awareness in order to -

🔹 and enhance cluster durability.

🔹 Ensure rapid leader failover in case of controller failures.

🔹 Optimize partition replication for multi-region or multi-rack deployments.

As KRaft continues to evolve, organizations should adopt dynamic, AI-assisted optimizations for better failover handling, metadata propagation, and rack-aware leadership election.

✅ Proper rack-aware configurations are no longer optional — they are a necessity for high-availability Kafka clusters.

items.title: Fine-Tuning Snowflake Roles — Creating A Comprehensive Read-Only Role
items.author: Noel
items.content:
Fine-Tuning Snowflake Roles — Creating A Comprehensive Read-Only Role

Snowflake’s Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system is a fundamental security mechanism that governs data access at a granular level. In a cloud data warehouse, misconfigured roles can lead to unintended data exposure, excessive privileges, or compliance violations. This makes it crucial to design roles carefully, ensuring that users have just enough permissions to perform their required tasks — nothing more.

One of the most common access control requirements is a read-only role that enables users to query data and view object metadata while preventing modifications, deletions, or unauthorized access. Without a properly configured read-only role, organizations risk accidental data modifications, inefficient permission management, and potential security loopholes.

The Need For ARead-Only Role in Snowflake

A is essential in Snowflake for several reasons -

✅ → Users can query and analyze data without the risk of accidental deletions or modifications.

✅ Enforcing Least Privilege → Reducing exposure by restricting permissions to only what is necessary.

✅ Improving Security & Compliance → Helps meet regulatory requirements by clearly defining access scopes.

✅ Streamlining User Management → A single, well-defined read-only role prevents redundant access assignments.

For scenarios where data analysts, auditors, business users, or external vendors need access to Snowflake data but should not modify schemas, tables, or objects, a read-only role ensures compliance and security while maintaining operational efficiency.

This article will provide a step-by-step guide to how our Snowflake specialists at Xinthe go about —

🔹 Designing A Least-Privilege Read-Only Role → Ensuring it covers all necessary Snowflake objects (tables, views, streams, policies, etc.).

🔹 Understanding Snowflake’s Hierarchical RBAC Model → How role inheritance works and its implications for read-only access.

🔹 Applying Security Best Practices In Role Configurations → Avoiding over-provisioning and ensuring compliance with best practices.

🔹 Automating Role Creation & Permission Assignment → Using SQL scripts for scalable, repeatable security policies.

When you’re done reading this article, you will have a fully operational, security-compliant, and scalable read-only role for your Snowflake environment.

Understanding Snowflake’s Role Hierarchy

  • Key Concepts In Snowflake RBAC

Snowflake’s Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model is designed to manage user privileges in a structured, hierarchical manner. Understanding these fundamental concepts is essential before creating a least-privilege read-only role.

🔹 → Individual accounts (human users or service accounts) that access Snowflake.

🔹 → Define what actions a user can perform on different Snowflake objects (databases, schemas, tables, views, etc.).

🔹 → Permissions assigned to roles at different levels (database, schema, or object).

🔹 → Every object has an owner, which controls who can access or modify it.

A read-only role must be configured carefully to avoid inheriting unnecessary write or admin privileges while still allowing access to view all required data objects.

  • How Role Inheritance Works In Snowflake

Snowflake roles follow a hierarchical structure, meaning that child roles automatically inherit all privileges of their parent roles. This structure simplifies access management but can also introduce security risks if roles with excessive permissions are inherited unintentionally.

Key Considerations For Read-Only Role Inheritance:

✅ Avoid Inheriting From Write/Admin Roles → The read-only role should be completely isolated from any role with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or ALTER permissions.

✅ Follow The Principle Of Least Privilege → Only grant the minimum necessary privileges required for read-only access.

✅ Ensure Hierarchy Consistency → Assign read-only roles at the appropriate level (database/schema) to prevent unnecessary cross-schema access.

  • Example Of Snowflake’s Role Hierarchy

A well-structured RBAC hierarchy ensures clear separation of duties while maintaining flexibility.

ACCOUNTADMIN ├── SYSADMIN
 │    ├── READ_WRITE_ROLE (granted privileges to modify data)
 │    ├── READ_ONLY_ROLE (restricted to SELECT queries)

Explanation Of The Hierarchy:

ACCOUNTADMIN: The highest privilege role, responsible for full Snowflake administration.

SYSADMIN: Typically used for database and schema administration.

READ_WRITE_ROLE: A custom role with SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE privileges.

READ_ONLY_ROLE: A custom role designed specifically for SELECT-only access.

SECURITYADMIN: Manages role assignments and grants without modifying data.

By defining roles clearly and isolating read-only access, organizations can prevent privilege escalation, enforce security best practices, and ensure compliance with data governance policies. 🚀

Defining Read-Only Access Scope

When defining a read-only role in Snowflake, it is essential to establish clear access boundaries to ensure that users can view but not modify data. A well-structured read-only role should adhere to the following principles -

✅ Restrict Access To Specific Databases & Schemas → Avoid blanket privileges across all objects unless necessary.

✅ Grant Query Access But Block Modifications → Allow SELECT, SHOW, and DESCRIBE privileges, but restrict INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and ALTER.

✅ Prevent Inheritance Of Unnecessary Privileges → Ensure the role does not inherit from roles with write or admin access.

  • Key Privileges For A Read-Only Role

A read-only role in Snowflake should be assigned the following minimal set of privileges to ensure it has visibility into the necessary database objects without the ability to modify them.

  • Example — Granting Read-Only Privileges To A Role

Step 1 — Create A Read-Only Role

CREATE ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

Step 2 — Grant Database & Schema Access

GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE my_database TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

Step 3 — Grant Table & View Access

GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON ALL VIEWS IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

Step 4 — Enable Metadata Visibility

GRANT SHOW ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT DESCRIBE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;
  • Ensuring Compliance With Least Privilege Access

By carefully scoping the read-only role to the necessary databases, schemas, and objects, organizations can -

✔ Enhance security by preventing unauthorized modifications.

✔ Simplify compliance with governance and audit policies.

✔ Optimize database performance by restricting access to relevant datasets only.

This structured least-privilege approach ensures that users have only the access they need — nothing more, nothing less. 🚀

Creating A Read-Only Role In Snowflake

  • Step 1 — Create The Read-Only Role

A dedicated read-only role ensures that users can query and view metadata but cannot modify any data or objects.

CREATE ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

📌 This command creates a new role that will later be assigned limited access privileges.

Step 2 — Assign Database & Schema Usage

To allow the role to reference objects within a specific database and schema, grant USAGE privileges.

GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE my_database TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ USAGE allows users to reference the database and schema but does not grant access to tables or views.

Step 3 — Grant Read-Only Access To Tables & Views

Explicitly grant SELECT privileges so that users assigned to this role can query tables and views.

GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON ALL VIEWS IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Users can now read from all existing tables and views but cannot modify them.

Step 4 — Ensure Access To Future Tables & Views

To automatically grant SELECT privileges on tables and views created in the future, use GRANT ON FUTURE statements.

GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE VIEWS IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Prevents manual intervention by ensuring new tables and views remain accessible to the role.

Step 5 — Assign The Role To Users

Finally, grant the role to specific users who require read-only access.

GRANT ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE TO USER analyst_user;

✅ User analyst_user can now query tables but cannot modify data or configurations.

  • Ensuring Security & Compliance

By implementing a structured read-only access policy, organizations can -

✔ Minimize the risk of accidental modifications to production data.

✔ Comply with regulatory & governance requirements by enforcing least-privilege principles.

✔ Simplify access management by creating a reusable role instead of granting privileges per user.

🚀 This structured role-based approach ensures data security while allowing analysts and stakeholders to retrieve insights efficiently.

Enhancing Security For Read-Only Roles In Snowflake

  • 1️⃣ Restricting Role Privileges

To prevent privilege escalation, a read-only role should not inherit high-level admin permissions.

✔ Do not assign it to SECURITYADMIN, SYSADMIN, or ACCOUNTADMIN

✔ Ensure it cannot modify existing privileges

Avoid Granting Ownership Or Modification Permissions

Users assigned to the read-only role should never be allowed to transfer ownership or modify objects.

🚨 Avoid granting the following privileges -

REVOKE MODIFY ON DATABASE my_database FROM ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;REVOKE OWNERSHIP ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema FROM ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Prevents role holders from modifying objects or reassigning permissions.

  • 2️⃣ Preventing Unauthorized Queries

By default, read-only users can execute SHOW statements to list all database objects.

If metadata access is not required, revoke this privilege to prevent users from exploring unauthorized objects.

REVOKE SHOW ON DATABASE my_database FROM ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;REVOKE SHOW ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema FROM ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Restricts visibility into system metadata, reducing exposure risk.

  • 3️⃣ Enforcing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

To strengthen access control, require users assigned to the read-only role to use MFA.

Enable MFA For Role-Specific Users

Snowflake allows MFA enforcement via network policies and identity provider (IdP) integrations (Okta, Azure AD, etc.).

ALTER USER analyst_user SET MFA_REQUIRED = TRUE;

✅ Adds an extra layer of authentication security to prevent unauthorized access.

Security Best Practices Summary

🔹  — Prevent admin-level roles from acquiring read-only privileges.

🔹  — Restrict metadata visibility when not required.

🔹  — Enforce multi-factor authentication for read-only users.

🚀 By implementing these security controls, organizations can minimize risks while maintaining controlled data access in Snowflake.

Auditing & Monitoring Role Usage In Snowflake

Ensuring proper monitoring and auditing of the READ_ONLY_ROLE is essential for compliance, security, and detecting privilege escalations. Snowflake provides built-in audit logs via the ACCOUNT_USAGE schema.

  • 1️⃣ Checking Role Assignments

To verify who has been assigned the READ_ONLY_ROLE, use -

SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Lists all users and roles that have been assigned the READ_ONLY_ROLE.

If you need to check which roles inherit privileges from READ_ONLY_ROLE, use -

SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Helps detect if higher-privileged roles have inherited read-only access.

  • 2️⃣ Tracking Query History

Monitor how and when the read-only role is used by querying Snowflake’s ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY -

SELECT     query_id,     role_name,     execution_status, FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
WHERE role_name = 'READ_ONLY_ROLE'
ORDER BY start_time DESC;

✅ Identifies potential misuse, such as unauthorized query attempts.

Use Cases For Query Auditing:

✔ Identify suspicious activity (e.g., excessive metadata queries).

✔ Ensure users are only performing read operations.

✔ Detect unauthorized long-running queries.

  • 3️⃣ Detecting Privilege Escalations

To track whether additional privileges have been granted to READ_ONLY_ROLE, use -

SELECT     role_name,     granted_by, FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES
WHERE role_name = 'READ_ONLY_ROLE';

✅ Helps detect unauthorized privilege escalations (e.g., granting MODIFY or OWNERSHIP).

Monitor For Suspicious Grants

If the READ_ONLY_ROLE is suddenly granted write privileges, this query will reveal when and by whom it was modified.

Security Best Practices For Auditing & Monitoring

🔹 Regularly check role assignments to ensure compliance.

🔹 Monitor query activity to detect unauthorized data access.

🔹 Track privilege changes to prevent unauthorized role escalations.

🚀 By implementing these audit strategies, organizations can maintain a robust and compliant access control policy in Snowflake.

Best Practices For Read-Only Role Management In Snowflake

Ensuring tight security and compliance for a read-only role requires following best practices in access control, monitoring, and data visibility.

  • 1️⃣ Follow The Principle Of Least Privilege (PoLP)

The Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) ensures users only have access to the minimum necessary data to perform their tasks.

🔹 Restrict read access to only the required schemas and tables, rather than granting database-wide privileges.

🔹 Avoid inheritance from roles with write or admin privileges to prevent privilege escalation.

Example — Granting Access To A Specific Schema Instead Of The Entire Database

GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA my_database.critical_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.critical_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ This ensures read-only access is granted only to relevant schemas and tables.

  • 2️⃣ Use Schema-Level Access Control

Instead of granting read access at the database level, follow a schema-specific access model.

✔ Better Control Over Permissions — Different schemas may contain different levels of sensitive data.

✔ Improved Security Compliance — Grants are specific to business requirements.

Example — Restricting Access To Only Certain Schemas

GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA my_database.finance_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.finance_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ This prevents unauthorized users from accessing unnecessary schemas.

  • 3️⃣ Monitor & Rotate Role Assignments

Snowflake users should not retain read access indefinitely. Periodic audits help enforce access control policies.

Regularly Audit User-Role Mappings

SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

Remove Inactive Users From The Role

REVOKE ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE FROM USER inactive_user;

✅ This prevents unnecessary long-term data exposure.

  • 4️⃣ Limit Visibility Of Sensitive Data

Even for read-only users, sensitive columns should be masked to ensure compliance with GDPR, HIPAA, or SOC 2 regulations.

Masking Sensitive Data Using Row Access Policies

To prevent users with READ_ONLY_ROLE from seeing sensitive data, we mask specific columns dynamically.

Step 1 — Create A Row Access Policy

CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY mask_sensitive_dataAS (user_role STRING) RETURNS STRING ->    WHEN user_role = 'READ_ONLY_ROLE' THEN 'MASKED'  END;

Step 2 — Apply The Policy To A Specific Column

ALTER TABLE my_database.my_schema.sensitive_tableMODIFY COLUMN sensitive_column SET ROW ACCESS POLICY mask_sensitive_data;

✅ This ensures that sensitive fields appear as “MASKED” for read-only users while allowing full visibility for authorized roles.

✔ Limit read-only access to only necessary schemas/tables.

✔ Grant access at the schema level instead of the entire database.

✔ Audit role assignments to ensure compliance.

✔ Mask sensitive data even for read-only users.

🚀 By following these best practices, organizations can enforce a secure, scalable, and compliance-friendly read-only access policy in Snowflake.

Advanced Configurations For Read-Only Roles In Snowflake

To enhance security, performance, and governance, advanced configurations can be applied to read-only roles in Snowflake. These configurations include time-based access control and resource monitoring to prevent excessive queries.

  • 1️⃣ Implementing Time-Based Access For Read-Only Roles

Certain users should have temporary read-only access, which should automatically expire after a defined period.

Solution: Using Snowflake Tasks to Auto-Revoke Access

Snowflake Tasks can be scheduled to revoke the role after a specific timeframe.

Example — Automatically Revoking Read-Only Role At Midnight

CREATE TASK revoke_read_only_accessWAREHOUSE = my_warehouse
SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 0 * * * UTC'  -- Runs daily at midnight UTC
AS
REVOKE ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE FROM USER analyst_user;

✅ This ensures temporary access does not persist longer than necessary, reducing security risks.

  • 2️⃣ Using Resource Monitors To Prevent Excessive Queries

Even though the read-only role cannot modify data, unrestricted querying can still consume resources and impact performance.

Solution — Define A Resource Monitor For Read-Only Users

Resource monitors enforce query quotas by suspending execution if a user exceeds allocated credits.

Example — Restricting Read-Only Users To 50 Credits Per Day

CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR read_only_monitorWITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 50  -- Limits daily credit consumption to 50TRIGGERS ON 90 PERCENT DO SUSPEND;

✅ This prevents excessive resource usage by read-only users.

  • 3️⃣ Enforcing Read-Only Access With Query Timeouts

To prevent long-running queries from consuming too many resources, set a timeout limit.

Example — Setting Query Timeout For Read-Only Role

ALTER SESSION SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 300;

✅ Ensures queries by read-only users do not exceed 5 minutes, preventing inefficient execution.

✔ Use automated role revocation for temporary access control.

✔ Apply resource monitors to prevent excessive queries.

✔ Set query timeouts to optimize performance.

🚀 With these advanced configurations, organizations can enforce secure, efficient, and scalable role management in Snowflake.

Troubleshooting Common Issues In Read-Only Role Configuration

Even with proper setup, read-only roles in Snowflake may face access issues, visibility problems, or excessive query consumption. Below are common issues and solutions to resolve them efficiently.

  • 1️⃣ Role Does Not Have Query Permissions

A user assigned to the read-only role encounters the following error when trying to query a table -

SQL Error: Insufficient privileges to operate on table 'my_table'

The role does not have SELECT privileges on the table or schema.

✅ Solution — Grant SELECT Privileges On Tables & Views

GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON ALL VIEWS IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

💡 Also ensure SELECT privileges are granted on future tables -

GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE VIEWS IN SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ This allows the role to read from both existing and newly created tables.

  • 2️⃣ User Can’t See Tables In The Database

The user assigned to the read-only role executes SHOW TABLES; but does not see any tables.

Even if a role has SELECT privileges, USAGE on the database and schema is required to list tables.

✅ Solution — Grant USAGE On Database & Schema

GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE my_database TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

💡 If metadata visibility is required, grant SHOW privileges -

GRANT SHOW ON DATABASE my_database TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;GRANT SHOW ON SCHEMA my_database.my_schema TO ROLE READ_ONLY_ROLE;

✅ Ensures the role can view schema structure but not modify anything.

  • 3️⃣ Excessive Query Usage From Read-Only Role

Read-only users consume excessive compute resources by running costly analytical queries.

Even though the role cannot modify data, it can still execute resource-intensive queries.

✅ Solution 1 — Limit Query Resource Usage With Resource Monitors

CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR read_only_monitorWITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 50TRIGGERS ON 90 PERCENT DO SUSPEND;

💡 Suspends execution if queries exceed 50 credits per day.

✅ Solution 2 — Set A Query Timeout To Prevent Long-Running Queries

ALTER SESSION SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 300;

💡 Automatically cancels any query running for more than 5 minutes.

✔ Grant SELECT privileges for query execution.

✔ Ensure USAGE privileges for database and schema visibility.

✔ Limit query execution using resource monitors and timeouts.

🚀 By following these troubleshooting steps, organizations can efficiently manage and secure read-only access in Snowflake.

A well-configured read-only role in Snowflake ensures security, compliance, and operational efficiency while preventing unauthorized data modifications. Through the leveraging of Snowflake’s RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) model, our Snowflake specialists at Xinthe are able to seamlessly define precise privileges and implement least-privilege access principles.

🚀 By implementing a well-defined read-only role strategy, you can now seriously thin about enhancing security, enforcing compliance, and maintaining a streamlined data access model in Snowflake.

items.content:
MySQL — How to Create a Table?

In MySQL, tables are used to store data in a structured format. In this article, we will learn how to create a table in MySQL.

Before we begin, you should have the following:

  • A database in which to create the table
  • Administrative privileges to create tables in the database

To create a new table in MySQL, follow these steps:

  1. Log in to the MySQL server using the following command:

Replace __username` with your MySQL username. You will be prompted to enter your password.

3. Once you are logged in, select the database in which you want to create the table:

Replace __dbname` with the name of the database in which you want to create the table.

4. Create a new table using the following command:

CREATE TABLE tablename (column1 datatype constraints,
column2 datatype constraints,
…
columnN datatype constraints
);

Replace __tablename with the name you want to give to your table. Replace __column1, __column2, and so on with the names of the columns you want to create. Replace __datatype with the data type of the column. Replace __constraints with any additional constraints you want to add, such as __NOT NULL or __UNIQUE`.

5. Verify that the table has been created by using the following command:

This will display a list of all the tables in the selected database, including the one you just created.
